proposals.title: Task-Dependent Reversal of Semantic Facilitation: An fMRI Investigation of Action and Object Event Processing
proposals.motivation: Semantic relatedness typically facilitates object recognition and event comprehension. However, Caramazza's recent work on a shared neural code for actions and object events suggests that the role of semantic information may be flexible and task-dependent. It remains unknown whether semantic relatedness can, under specific task demands, become a source of interference rather than facilitation, challenging the standard view of semantic processing as uniformly beneficial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantic relatedness will facilitate performance in a categorization task but will impair performance in a specific individuation task (e.g., verifying a specific action detail), due to competition between co-activated, related event representations in lateral occipito-temporal cortex.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to test participants on visually presented action and object event videos. In a blocked design, we will contrast two tasks: 1) a coarse categorization task (e.g., 'Is this a cooking event?') and 2) a fine-grained individuation task (e.g., 'Did the agent use a whisk?'). Stimuli will be pairs of semantically related (e.g., whisking, stirring) or unrelated events. We will analyze BOLD signals in regions of interest (lateral occipital complex, posterior middle temporal gyrus) using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode task-relevant information and assess representational similarity under the two conditions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide novel evidence that the functional role of semantic information is not fixed but is dynamically modulated by task goals. It will directly link Caramazza's theory of a shared action-object event code to the cognitive control of semantic representations, offering a more nuanced model of how lateral occipito-temporal cortex supports event understanding.
proposals.novelty: While semantic interference is known in lexical processing (e.g., Stroop), demonstrating its inversion in the domain of complex visual events, and linking it to a shift from categorization to individuation, is a novel and non-obvious prediction of a flexible neural event code.

proposals.title: Neural Dissociation of Agentive and Linguistic Forces in Simultaneous Interpreting: An fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: Simultaneous interpreting (SI) is a uniquely demanding cognitive event requiring concurrent language comprehension and production under severe time constraints. While the neural bases of language are well-studied, the specific cortical systems that support the dynamic transformation of conceptual and syntactic structures during real-time SI remain poorly understood. This study will directly test how the brain integrates agentive (speaker-intent) and linguistic forces during this complex action, bridging Caramazza's work on event representation with models of bilingual processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful SI will engage a distinct fronto-temporal-parietal network, with dorsal stream regions (including posterior superior temporal sulcus and inferior parietal lobule) specifically encoding the transformation of event structure between languages, beyond the activity observed in non-interpreting bilingual tasks.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution (3T) fMRI to compare brain activity in professional interpreters across three conditions: (1) Simultaneous Interpreting (L1 to L2), (2) Shadowing (repeating L1 speech), and (3) Delayed Translation. Stimuli will be controlled narratives varying in agentive and physical event complexity (e.g., 'the author convinced the critic' vs. 'the ball rolled'). Data analysis will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) to identify regions where neural patterns for event categories are preserved across input and output languages, specifically during the SI condition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first neural map of event structure transformation during SI, identifying core regions for syntactic-semantic remapping. It will extend the 'shared neural code' framework from object-directed actions to complex linguistic actions, offering a mechanistic account of how agentive and physical forces are processed in a communicative context. The findings will constrain neurocognitive models of bilingualism and expert skill.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior neuroimaging studies of translation, this design isolates the neural processes of online structure conversion by contrasting SI with shadowing and delayed translation, and directly tests for a transformation-specific neural code for event semantics as proposed in Caramazza's recent work.

proposals.title: Mapping the Computational Strategy Space: A Formal Model of Processing Signatures for Object and Event Recognition
proposals.motivation: Current cognitive neuroscience often describes processing strategies (e.g., holistic vs. analytic) as qualitative poles on a continuum, lacking formal, falsifiable definitions. This limits rigorous comparison across agents (humans, AI, patients) and fails to specify the underlying computational trade-offs. Grounded in Caramazza's pre-2026 work on shared neural codes for actions/objects and shape-centered representations, this proposal seeks to formalize these intuitions.
proposals.hypothesis: That the processing strategies used for visual recognition and event understanding can be quantified as points in a low-dimensional manifold defined by core computational metrics, and that the distribution of strategies across agents will reveal a structured space with identifiable clusters and gaps, rather than a simple continuum.
proposals.method: We will define metrics like invariance learning rate (from adaptation paradigms) and working memory recursion depth (from sequential masking tasks). A large-scale benchmark will collect these metrics from humans, neurological patients (e.g., with lateral occipital cortex lesions), and diverse AI models (CNNs, Vision Transformers, capsule networks). We will then apply non-linear manifold learning techniques (e.g., t-SNE, UMAP) to the high-dimensional metric space to empirically map the strategy landscape and test for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first formal, quantifiable model of cognitive processing strategies, enabling direct, falsifiable tests of the continuum hypothesis. It will link high-level behavioral phenotypes to low-level computational primitives, offering a new framework for interpreting neural data (e.g., from action-selective cortex) and explaining deficits in patient populations.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond qualitative descriptions to a quantifiable, computational-geometric model of processing strategies is a significant advance. The systematic comparison across biological and artificial agents using a common formal language is a novel and powerful approach to understanding core principles of intelligence.

proposals.title: Adversarial Task Design to Dissociate Task Demands from Architectural Constraints in the Neural Representation of Actions and Objects
proposals.motivation: A central debate in cognitive neuroscience concerns whether behavioral dissociations (e.g., in object vs. action recognition) reflect distinct neural architectures or flexible processing driven by task demands. Pre-2026 work, including Caramazza's on shared neural codes for action physics and object events, highlights this tension but lacks a formal framework for strong, falsifiable tests. Resolving this is critical for understanding the fundamental organization of the visual and conceptual systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, flexible computational architecture, modulated by task-specific demands, can account for observed dissociations in action and object processing better than models positing multiple, independent architectural modules.
proposals.method: We will construct two competing computational models: a single-architecture model (e.g., a deep neural network with task-specific readout layers) and a multiple-architecture model (e.g., a modular network with dedicated pathways). Using formal adversarial optimization (e.g., generative adversarial network principles), we will design novel behavioral and fMRI tasks that maximally differentiate their predictions. These adversarial tasks will be implemented with human participants, comparing behavioral performance and neural patterns (e.g., multivoxel pattern analysis in lateral occipitotemporal cortex) to model outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, model-driven method to move beyond correlational evidence in architecture debates. It will yield concrete empirical evidence for or against distinct architectural components in high-level vision, directly informing theories of category-specific representation. The adversarial design framework itself will be a novel tool for theory testing in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal adversarial design—common in machine learning—to generate critical cognitive neuroscience experiments, creating a strong inferential test that existing methods (like neuroimaging correlations or single-dissociation studies) cannot provide.

proposals.title: Task-Dependent Modulation of Shared Representational Codes for Objects and Events: An fMRI and Representational Similarity Analysis Study
proposals.motivation: Neuropsychological and neuroimaging studies often report dissociations between the processing of objects and events, suggesting distinct cognitive and neural architectures. However, Caramazza's recent work (pre-2026) posits a shared neural code for the physics of actions and object events. This raises a critical, unresolved question: whether observed dissociations reflect fundamental architectural separations or are epiphenomena of specific task demands that differentially engage a common representational substrate.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representations for objects and events in lateral occipito-temporal cortex (LOTC) are fundamentally shared but are selectively modulated by task context, such that tasks emphasizing agentive versus physical properties will elicit patterns of activity that mimic functional dissociations.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution fMRI and Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) with a 2x2 factorial design: Stimulus Type (object, event) x Task Demand (agentive judgment, physical judgment). Participants will view identical visual stimuli (e.g., a hand grasping a cup) under different instructional sets. We will construct neural representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) from LOTC and ventral temporal cortex and compare them to computational model RDMs derived from pre-2026 deep convolutional neural networks (e.g., ResNet-50) and physics-grounded models of force dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct evidence for a task-dependent account of object/event processing dissociations, challenging modular architectural interpretations. It will refine the 'shared neural code' hypothesis by specifying how task goals dynamically shape information readout from common representational formats in high-level visual cortex.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified shared representations, no study has systematically manipulated task demands on identical stimuli to test if this manipulation alone can produce the classic neural and behavioral dissociations cited as evidence for separate systems.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Mapping of Cognitive-Linguistic Recovery Trajectories onto Dynamic Neural Representational Spaces in Post-Stroke Aphasia
proposals.motivation: Post-stroke aphasia recovery is highly variable, and traditional group-level analyses obscure individual neuroplasticity pathways. Pre-2026, longitudinal neuroimaging studies in aphasia are sparse and often test pre-defined anatomical models rather than discovering data-driven profiles linking cognitive and neural change. A high-dimensional, multi-timescale approach is needed to uncover the fundamental mechanisms linking the recovery of object, action, and event concepts to the reorganization of their neural representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unsupervised clustering of longitudinal cognitive-linguistic data will reveal distinct recovery phenotypes that are specifically predicted by the rate and topology of dynamic changes in the neural representational structure for objects and actions within the lateral temporal cortex and broader language network.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 individuals with post-stroke aphasia for intensive longitudinal assessment over 12 months, with bi-weekly cognitive-linguistic testing (including object/action naming, event description) and monthly multimodal MRI (structural, resting-state, and task-based fMRI). Unsupervised learning (e.g., variational autoencoders for dimensionality reduction followed by Gaussian Mixture Models) will identify behavioral trajectory clusters. We will then model neural trajectories using representational similarity analysis (RSA) on fMRI data from object and action perception tasks, and dynamic functional connectivity, to test for phenotype-specific neural signatures.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first data-driven taxonomy of aphasia recovery trajectories grounded in both behavior and neural dynamics. It will directly test how the recovery of conceptual domains studied by Caramazza (objects, actions, events) is supported by the re-organization of specific cortical representations and networks, moving beyond static lesion-deficit maps to a mechanistic model of neuroplasticity.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying unsupervised computational phenotyping to longitudinal aphasia data and explicitly linking these behavioral phenotypes to the dynamics of neural representational spaces, a direct extension of Caramazza's work on the neural code for objects and actions into the domain of recovery.

proposals.title: Linking Neural Dynamics to Signal Detection Parameters in the Perception of Action and Object Events
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience has long sought neural correlates of different processing modes, such as algorithmic versus heuristic strategies. However, current approaches often rely on coarse behavioral contrasts, lacking a formal computational framework to link neural activity to the fundamental perceptual decision parameters of sensitivity and bias. This gap limits our ability to understand how distinct neural systems implement different cognitive strategies during complex visual tasks like event perception.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that shifts from algorithmic to heuristic processing during the perception of action and object events will be characterized by dissociable neural signatures that map directly onto Signal Detection Theory (SDT) parameters: frontal-parietal theta-band coherence will track changes in perceptual sensitivity (d'), while pupil-linked arousal and ventromedial prefrontal activity will predict shifts in response criterion (c).
proposals.method: We will design visual tasks where participants categorize dynamic events (e.g., tool-use actions vs. object collisions), systematically manipulating task demands to induce algorithmic (effortful, rule-based) or heuristic (fast, similarity-based) processing. Behavioral data will be modeled with SDT to extract trial-by-trial estimates of d' and criterion. Concurrent high-density EEG and pupillometry will be recorded. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) on EEG time-frequency data and pupil dilation responses to identify neural features that predict the SDT parameters. A follow-up fMRI study will localize the cortical sources of these predictive signals, focusing on lateral occipitotemporal cortex for action/object representations and prefrontal regions for criterion setting.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, formal model linking specific neural dynamics to the core computational parameters of perceptual decisions. It will advance Caramazza's research on event representation by showing how the neural code for actions and object physics interfaces with decision systems. The findings will establish a new framework for dissociating cognitive states using a unified psychophysiological-SDT approach.
proposals.novelty: While SDT is a standard behavioral tool, its parameters have not been rigorously mapped onto specific, time-resolved neural and physiological signals to dissociate cognitive modes. This proposal uniquely integrates high-temporal-resolution physiology with the computational rigor of SDT to move beyond correlation to a mechanistic model of how neural systems implement sensitivity and bias.

proposals.title: Triangulating the Neural Dynamics of Event Comprehension: A Dual-Task ERP and Probe RT Study of Algorithmic vs. Heuristic Processing
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in cognitive neuroscience, including work by Caramazza's group, has identified distinct neural correlates for processing object-related actions and motion events. However, ERP components like the N400 and P600 are correlational markers, making it difficult to causally link them to specific cognitive operations such as algorithmic parsing versus heuristic prediction during language comprehension. A method is needed to behaviorally anchor these neural signatures in real-time processing demands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the engagement of algorithmic (rule-based) versus heuristic (prediction-based) comprehension strategies will be disambiguated by distinct spatiotemporal profiles in the ERP signal that are systematically coupled with concurrent, time-resolved behavioral measures of cognitive load from a secondary probe task.
proposals.method: Participants will read sentences describing physical and agentive events (e.g., 'The vase shattered' vs. 'The chef shattered the vase') while EEG is recorded. At critical, pre-determined latencies post-stimulus onset (e.g., at verb onset, post-verb), processing will be randomly interrupted by simple auditory or visual probes requiring a speeded button press. We will employ linear mixed-effects models to analyze the probe reaction time (RT) time-series as a function of sentence type and probe latency. This RT profile will serve as an independent behavioral index of processing load to be directly correlated with the amplitude and latency of ERP components (N400, P600, LAN) using multivariate pattern analysis and time-frequency decomposition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic account of how event semantics are constructed in real-time, moving beyond neural correlations to a framework where ERP signatures are validated by concurrent behavioral resource allocation. It will directly inform theories of the 'shared neural code' for actions and events by specifying the temporal dynamics of the underlying computational processes.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a secondary probe RT task within an ERP paradigm to create a time-resolved behavioral metric of cognitive load is a novel methodological synthesis. This allows for the triangulation of neural and behavioral data streams to disambiguate processing modes, a significant advance over traditional single-task ERP studies.

proposals.title: Resolving Cognitive Modes in Event Comprehension: A Time-Frequency Analysis of ERP Signatures
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the brain dynamically switches between algorithmic (controlled) and heuristic (predictive) processing modes during event comprehension remains a central challenge in cognitive neuroscience. Traditional ERP analysis, focusing on component latencies and amplitudes, lacks the temporal resolution to track these rapid, potentially overlapping cognitive dynamics. This limits our ability to test theories about the neural code for actions and events, a core theme in Caramazza's research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct cognitive modes in event comprehension are characterized by separable, time-evolving oscillatory signatures in the EEG, specifically that algorithmic processing will be indexed by increased theta-band power and fronto-parietal phase coherence, while heuristic processing will be indexed by alpha/beta-band suppression and distinct coherence patterns.
proposals.method: We will adapt signal processing techniques from seismology, applying continuous wavelet transforms to single-trial EEG data recorded during tasks that manipulate demands for algorithmic vs. heuristic processing of action and motion events (e.g., anomalous vs. predictable event sequences). This time-frequency decomposition will allow us to extract trial-by-trial measures of oscillatory power and inter-regional phase-locking value (PLV) within theta (4-8 Hz), alpha (8-12 Hz), and beta (15-30 Hz) bands. These spectral features will be modeled as time-series to identify their precise onset, duration, and relationship to stimulus variables and behavioral responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-temporal-resolution account of the dynamic neural states underlying event comprehension, moving beyond static ERP components. It will directly contribute to models of how agentive and physical forces are neurally represented by linking specific processing modes to spectro-temporal signatures. The method establishes a new analysis framework for dissecting real-time cognitive operations.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct conceptual and technical analogy from seismology to cognitive EEG analysis, using time-frequency resolution to disentangle overlapping 'cognitive wave modes'—an approach not standard in the ERP literature on event perception pre-2026. This offers a more dynamic view than traditional ERP component analysis alone.

proposals.title: Dual-Route Parsing of Narrative Events: A Computational Model of Algorithmic and Heuristic Discourse Comprehension
proposals.motivation: Understanding how the brain parses multi-sentence narratives remains a core challenge in cognitive neuroscience. Pre-2026 work on object and event semantics suggests distinct neural codes for agentive and physical forces, but it is unclear how these representations are integrated at the discourse level. A computational dual-route framework can bridge this gap by providing a mechanistic account of how structured, compositional processing and fast, pattern-based comprehension interact during naturalistic language understanding.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that discourse comprehension involves the interaction of an algorithmic route, which builds event structures based on compositional rules and agentive/physical forces, and a heuristic route, which relies on statistical co-occurrences and schema-based expectations, with the dominance of each route modulated by discourse coherence and cognitive load.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid neuro-symbolic computational model. The algorithmic route will be implemented as a structured, incremental parser informed by formal semantics of events and forces. The heuristic route will be a fine-tuned transformer model (e.g., BERT or GPT-2) trained on narrative corpora to predict next events. The model's internal dynamics and outputs (e.g., surprisal, coherence judgments) will generate testable predictions for human behavioral (self-paced reading, comprehension questions) and neural (fMRI adaptation paradigms in lateral temporal and prefrontal cortex) experiments using carefully constructed narrative stimuli.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated computational model directly linking dual-route parsing theories to discourse-level event comprehension. It will generate novel, quantitative predictions for dissociable neural substrates, extending the neural logic of action/event representation to the narrative domain. The validated framework will offer a principled explanation for variability in comprehension under different cognitive demands.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally modeling the dual-route theory at the discourse scale and explicitly grounding the algorithmic route in the neural representation of physical and agentive forces, a synthesis not yet attempted in pre-2026 cognitive computational modeling.

proposals.title: Beyond Neutral Baselines: The Role of Object Affordances and Event Structure in Attentional Capture
proposals.motivation: In cognitive neuroscience, neutral stimuli (e.g., scrambled objects, geometric shapes) are routinely used as a non-salient baseline to isolate neural responses to meaningful stimuli. This assumes 'neutrality' is a universal property, independent of task and observer. However, pre-2026 work from Caramazza's lab shows that object and event representations are inherently linked to action and physical forces, suggesting that a stimulus's potential for interaction (its affordance) may inherently modulate attention, even in passive viewing. This challenges the validity of a one-size-fits-all neutral baseline.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that stimuli traditionally labeled as 'neutral' are not attentionally inert; their capacity to capture attention is systematically modulated by low-level physical properties that imply potential for action or event structure, thereby contaminating baseline comparisons in studies of object and event perception.
proposals.method: We will combine fMRI adaptation and multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) in lateral occipital and posterior parietal cortex. Participants will perform a rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) task where traditional 'neutral' stimuli (scrambled objects, simple shapes) and meaningful objects are presented as distractors. Crucially, we will parametrically manipulate the low-level physical features of neutral stimuli (e.g., oriented contours, implied motion cues) based on principles from the 'neural code for physics of actions'. We will test whether these features predict behavioral measures of attentional capture (missed targets) and neural discriminability patterns, isolating feature dimensions that confer salience.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, empirically grounded critique of baseline selection in attention and perception research. It will directly link the literature on attentional capture to the neural representation of object affordances and event physics, offering a new framework for designing controlled experiments. The findings could lead to revised interpretations of prior fMRI studies comparing categories against 'neutral' baselines.
proposals.novelty: While the problem of baseline selection is known, our approach is novel in directly testing the assumption of neutrality by applying a theoretical framework from object/event neuroscience—specifically, the coding of physical and agentive forces—to the domain of low-level attentional capture.

proposals.title: Neural Dynamics of Pre-attentive Selection and Amplification in the Perception of Action-Relevant Object Events
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work, shows a shared neural code for action kinematics and object events, implicating lateral occipitotemporal cortex (LOTC). However, the precise spatiotemporal dynamics of the subprocesses that gate this shared representation—specifically pre-attentive selection, reorienting, and sensory amplification—remain poorly resolved. Understanding these dynamics is critical for linking early visual processing to high-level conceptual representations of events.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, temporally cascading neural signatures in LOTC and frontoparietal networks will be observed for (1) pre-attentive filtering of action-relevant visual features, (2) reorienting based on agentive cues, and (3) context-dependent sensory amplification of event representations.
proposals.method: We will combine high-density EEG (256-channel) and fMRI (3T) in a within-subjects design. Participants will view dynamic stimuli of objects undergoing physical events (e.g., breaking) and agent-driven actions (e.g., throwing). We will employ rapid serial visual presentation (RSVP) with attentional cueing and oddball paradigms. EEG data will be analyzed using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and time-frequency decomposition to track the millisecond evolution of event representations. fMRI data will be analyzed with multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and effective connectivity (Dynamic Causal Modeling) to localize and model the information flow between LOTC and frontoparietal attention networks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated high-resolution spatiotemporal map of the attentional subprocesses that shape event perception in LOTC. It will directly test whether the 'shared neural code' for actions and physical events is established pre-attentively or is a product of later amplification, refining models of the visual-cognitive hierarchy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified shared representations, this study uniquely targets the specific, dissociable neural mechanisms that gate access to this shared code, using a temporally precise multimodal imaging approach not previously applied to this question in Caramazza's framework.

proposals.title: Decoding the Neural Dynamics of Emotion-Attention Interactions: A Multivariate Pattern Analysis of EEG and fMRI Data During Naturalistic Event Perception
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, research in Caramazza's lab has established that the brain represents the physics of actions and events in a shared neural code within lateral occipito-temporal cortex. However, how emotional content, a potent attentional modulator, interacts with these shape- and motion-based representations during event perception remains poorly understood. Decoding the specific emotional category capturing attention from neural patterns is crucial for a complete model of how we parse the visual world.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate patterns of neural activity in high-level visual and attention-related cortical regions will reliably distinguish between event categories defined by their dominant emotional valence (e.g., threatening, rewarding, neutral), and that the decoding accuracy of these emotional categories will predict behavioral measures of attentional capture.
proposals.method: We will collect simultaneous EEG-fMRI data while participants view short video clips of naturalistic object-directed actions with varying emotional contexts. We will apply multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to fMRI data, using linear support vector machines (SVMs) to classify emotional categories from activity in regions of interest (lateral occipital complex, posterior superior temporal sulcus, amygdala). For EEG, we will use temporal decoding approaches (e.g., linear discriminant analysis on time-frequency features) to track the millisecond dynamics of emotional category discrimination. Cross-modal decoding will test for shared information between EEG patterns and fMRI patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly extend Caramazza's framework by mapping how agentive/physical forces and emotional forces are integrated in the neural representation of events. It will provide a novel, data-driven model of how emotion gates attention during action perception, linking high-level vision with affective neuroscience. The results will constrain theories of event perception by quantifying the informational content for emotion in event-specific neural codes.
proposals.novelty: While MVPA has been used to decode object and action categories, its application to decode the *emotional category* of dynamic, naturalistic events from neural patterns is novel. The integration of EEG temporal resolution with fMRI spatial localization to track the cascade of emotion-attention interactions during event perception represents a significant methodological advance over prior univariate studies.

proposals.title: Neural Asymmetry in Lexical Semantics: Right Hemisphere Contributions to the Representation of Action and Event Words
proposals.motivation: Classical neurolinguistics posits left hemisphere (LH) dominance for all word processing. However, pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work, shows the right hemisphere (RH) is crucial for processing visual events, spatial relations, and coarse semantic information. This creates a paradox: if the RH is fundamental for representing the physics and kinematics of actions and events, might it also play a constitutive role in processing words whose meanings are grounded in these very representations?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the right hemisphere, particularly regions in lateral occipitotemporal and parietal cortex involved in action and event perception, makes fundamental, non-compensatory contributions to the neural representation and processing of concrete action verbs and nominalized event words, challenging the assumption of uniform LH dominance.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA) in neurologically intact participants. Stimuli will include action verbs (e.g., 'throw', 'collapse'), object nouns, and event nouns, matched for frequency. We will test if neural patterns for action/event words in RH regions (e.g., right lateral occipital complex, posterior middle temporal gyrus) show significant representational similarity to patterns evoked by viewing the corresponding dynamic action and physical event videos (from Caramazza's prior work). A complementary voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping (VLSM) study in right-hemisphere stroke patients will assess causal links to semantic deficits for these word types.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct evidence linking RH event-perception systems to specific lexical-semantic representations, moving beyond coarse RH contributions. It will refine neurobiological models of the language network by specifying how cortical asymmetries are determined by semantic content, not just linguistic modality. The findings could explain residual semantic capacities in some aphasias.
proposals.novelty: While RH contributions to language are known, the proposal uniquely tests a principled, content-based prediction derived from perception research: that words denoting observable, physically-grounded events rely on RH perceptual-motor systems as fundamentally as they do on LH linguistic systems.

proposals.title: A Continuum of Concreteness: Graded Neural Representations of Object and Event Concepts in Lateral Occipitotemporal Cortex
proposals.motivation: The neural representation of conceptual knowledge is often dichotomized into abstract and concrete domains, linked to distinct brain networks. However, this binary view is challenged by concepts that blend sensory-motor and abstract features (e.g., 'grasp' vs. 'justice'). A continuum model of concreteness, grounded in the graded integration of physical and agentive forces, may better explain the organization of conceptual knowledge in high-level visual and semantic regions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the lateral occipitotemporal cortex (LOTC), a key region for representing object-directed actions and physical events, will exhibit a graded, continuous neural response pattern that tracks a behavioral concreteness continuum, rather than a binary abstract-concrete distinction.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to measure neural responses in LOTC and language regions (e.g., ATL) to a parametric stimulus set of words and short phrases spanning a concreteness continuum. This continuum will be defined by behavioral ratings and a computational model integrating pre-2026 semantic feature norms (e.g., Lancaster Sensorimotor Norms) with action kinematics and physical force dynamics derived from Caramazza's prior work. Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) will test if the neural representational geometry in LOTC correlates with a continuous, multi-dimensional model of concreteness more strongly than with a binary abstract-concrete model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, graded neural account of conceptual representation, directly linking the physics of actions/events to the semantics of language. It will challenge classical neuroanatomical models of semantic memory and offer a unifying framework for understanding how object, action, and event knowledge are integrated along a concreteness continuum.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified shared codes for action physics and event perception, applying this framework to deconstruct the abstract-concrete dichotomy in conceptual representation is novel. It moves beyond identifying category-specific regions to characterizing a fundamental organizing principle (concreteness) that cuts across traditional category boundaries.

proposals.title: Neural Topography of Modifiers and Relators: A PET Study of Adjectives, Adverbs, and Prepositions
proposals.motivation: Neurocognitive models of language, largely based on nouns and verbs, provide an incomplete picture of semantic representation. Adjectives (e.g., 'red'), adverbs ('quickly'), and prepositions ('above') encode critical perceptual, action-related, and spatial-relational properties that are central to object and event understanding. Mapping their neural correlates is essential for a comprehensive theory of how language interfaces with visual and motor systems, a core theme in Caramazza's research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representation of these word classes will fractionate according to their core semantic content: adjectives will engage ventral-temporal object-property regions, adverbs will engage dorsal action-related networks, and prepositions will engage parietal spatial systems, extending the logic of category-specific semantic organization.
proposals.method: We will use Positron Emission Tomography (PET) to measure regional cerebral blood flow in participants performing a covert semantic judgment task on visually presented words. The design will adapt established paradigms from Caramazza's lab, contrasting multiple word classes (nouns, verbs, adjectives, adverbs, prepositions) in a factorial design. Data analysis will employ Statistical Parametric Mapping (SPM12) for whole-brain contrasts and region-of-interest (ROI) analyses focused on ventral-temporal, lateral occipito-temporal, and intraparietal sulcus regions, informed by prior work on object and action representation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic PET-based neural map of major, understudied word classes, directly testing whether their representations are grounded in distinct perceptual and motor systems. It will bridge research on the neural representation of objects, actions, and events with the linguistics of modification and spatial relations, offering a more complete functional anatomy of the semantic system.
proposals.novelty: While prior neuroimaging has contrasted nouns and verbs, a systematic investigation of adjectives, adverbs, and prepositions within the same experimental framework, using PET's superior signal for localization in ventral and medial temporal lobes, is novel. It applies the representational logic of 'neural reference frames' from physical event perception directly to closed-class linguistic elements.

proposals.title: Convergent Neural Evidence for Event Representation: A Direct Integration of Lesion-Symptom Mapping and PET Activation in Aphasia
proposals.motivation: Understanding the neural basis of event knowledge, which integrates object, action, and force information, is a central challenge in cognitive neuroscience. Pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work, uses lesion studies (e.g., in aphasia) and functional neuroimaging (e.g., PET) separately, but these methods have complementary strengths and weaknesses. A direct, quantitative integration of these data types is needed to provide convergent, causal evidence for the neural structures critical for event processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that brain regions identified as critical for processing motion events (e.g., agentive vs. physical forces) by lesion-symptom mapping in chronic stroke patients will show significant spatial convergence with regions activated during event comprehension tasks in a PET study with healthy participants.
proposals.method: We will analyze high-resolution structural MRI and detailed behavioral data (e.g., event description, force judgment) from a well-characterized cohort of individuals with left-hemisphere stroke and aphasia. Voxel-based lesion-symptom mapping (VLSM) will identify lesion sites associated with specific event processing deficits. Separately, we will analyze existing or newly acquired 3D H2[15O] PET data from healthy controls performing matched event comprehension tasks. Convergence will be tested using a novel multivariate pattern analysis framework that directly compares the spatial topography of lesion-deficit maps and PET activation maps, employing permutation-based statistical tests to assess significance beyond chance anatomical overlap.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, methodologically rigorous model for integrating causal lesion data with functional activation maps. It will yield a more definitive map of the neural substrates necessary for event representation, clarifying the roles of regions in the lateral temporal and inferior frontal cortex. The findings will bridge the gap between neuropsychological models of semantic memory and functional neuroimaging of event comprehension.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies compare results across methods, this proposal implements a direct, voxel-wise statistical integration of lesion and PET data within the same analytical space. This moves beyond qualitative comparison to a quantitative test of convergence, specifically targeting the neural code for actions and events as outlined in Caramazza's recent work.

proposals.title: Mapping the Neural Geometry of Event Semantics: A Computational Model of Action and Object Representation in Lateral Occipitotemporal Cortex
proposals.motivation: Caramazza's recent work demonstrates that lateral occipitotemporal cortex (LOTC) encodes a shared neural code for the physics of actions and object events, integrating agentive and physical forces. However, the computational principles that organize this high-level semantic space remain unclear. Distributional semantic models, which capture word meaning from co-occurrence statistics, offer a powerful, pre-2026 method to test whether the statistical structure of language predicts the organization of event-related neural representations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representational geometry for verbs and nouns denoting actions and object events in LOTC can be predicted by their positions in a high-dimensional semantic vector space derived from large-scale text corpora, with distinct dimensions aligning with agentive versus physical force dynamics.
proposals.method: We will collect fMRI data while participants read verbs and nouns from Caramazza's established paradigms (e.g., *cut*, *roll*, *hammer*, *ball*). Neural representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) for these stimuli will be constructed from LOTC activation patterns. Concurrently, we will generate model RDMs using distributional semantic vectors from pre-trained models (e.g., GloVe, word2vec) and topic models (LDA) applied to large text corpora. Predictive power will be assessed using representational similarity analysis (RSA) and cross-validated linear encoding models that map semantic features to voxel-wise patterns, specifically testing for separate contributions of action-dynamic and object-property semantic subspaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational link between linguistic statistics and the neural representation of events, moving beyond descriptive mapping to a predictive model. It will test whether the 'shared neural code' for actions and objects is structured by the statistical regularities of how event concepts are used in language, offering a mechanistic account of semantic organization in high-level visual cortex.
proposals.novelty: While distributional models have been used for concrete nouns, their application to predict neural activity for dynamic event concepts, particularly integrating the distinct roles of agentive and physical forces as highlighted in Caramazza's work, is a novel synthesis of computational linguistics and cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: Direct Neural Dynamics of Event and Object Word Recognition: An iEEG Investigation of the Lateral Occipital-Temporal Cortex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work, posits that the lateral occipital-temporal cortex (LOTC) is a critical hub for representing both object shape and action/event semantics, suggesting a shared neural code for the physics of actions and object events. However, the precise spatiotemporal dynamics of how these distinct but related conceptual features are accessed during word reading remain poorly resolved, limited by the coarse resolution of fMRI or scalp EEG. Understanding this neural chronology is essential for refining models of the visual and semantic lexicon.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during single-word reading, distinct but overlapping neural populations within the LOTC will exhibit dissociable, temporally precise activation profiles: object-related words will preferentially engage more posterior LOTC sites earlier (~150-250ms), while event/action-related words will recruit more anterior LOTC sites slightly later (~200-300ms), reflecting a cascading access from structural to dynamic semantic features.
proposals.method: We will record intracranial EEG (iEEG) from electrodes implanted in the LOTC of epilepsy patients performing a word recognition task. Stimuli will include carefully matched nouns denoting manipulable objects (e.g., 'hammer') and verbs denoting physical actions (e.g., 'pound'). Neural data will be analyzed for high-frequency broadband activity (70-150 Hz) as a marker of local cortical processing. We will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and time-resolved decoding algorithms (e.g., linear support vector machines) to track the distinctiveness of neural representations for object vs. event words across time, and use source-localized current source density estimates to map spatial gradients of activation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, high-resolution map of the millisecond-scale dynamics of conceptual access in the LOTC during reading. It will critically test the 'shared neural code' model by revealing whether object and event semantics are processed serially, in parallel, or via integrated populations. The findings will constrain neurocomputational models of the visual word form area and conceptual representation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified LOTC regions for objects and actions, the novel combination of iEEG's spatiotemporal precision with multivariate decoding during a linguistic task will uniquely reveal the real-time transformation of orthographic input into distinct conceptual representations, moving beyond correlational localization to mechanistic dynamics.

proposals.title: Right Hemisphere Specialization for Event and Action Categories: A Lesion-Deficit and fMRI Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuropsychological and neuroimaging research has established a strong left hemisphere dominance for conceptual and linguistic processing, including for action semantics. However, Caramazza's recent work on a shared neural code for action physics and object events suggests a more distributed system. The role of the right hemisphere in conceptual representation, particularly for non-linguistic, dynamic event categories, remains underspecified and potentially critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lesions to the right anterior temporal lobe and temporo-parietal junction will produce specific deficits in the perceptual and conceptual processing of complex, non-agentive physical events (e.g., 'shattering,' 'flowing') and spatially-defined actions, complementing the left-lateralized deficits for tool-related and agentive actions.
proposals.method: We will test patients with focal right hemisphere lesions and matched controls on a battery of tasks probing event perception and conceptual knowledge. Tasks will include forced-choice matching of dynamic visual events (videos) to their outcomes or to verbs, and similarity judgments for action pairs. We will complement this with fMRI in healthy participants using multivoxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to identify regions in the right lateral temporal cortex whose activity patterns distinguish between event categories (e.g., containment vs. propulsion events) independent of low-level visual features.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the canonical left-lateralized model of conceptual representation by demonstrating a principled, category-specific division of labor between hemispheres. It will directly link the neural representation of physical events to a right-lateralized network, offering a more complete neural architecture for event cognition that integrates Caramazza's 'physics of actions' framework with hemispheric specialization.
proposals.novelty: While left hemisphere category-specific deficits are well-documented, systematically testing for complementary right hemisphere deficits for event categories inverts a core assumption in cognitive neuropsychology. It moves beyond language to test conceptual organization in the visual-dynamic domain where right hemisphere processing may be paramount.

proposals.title: Dynamic Reorganization of Category-Selective Cortex: Testing Plasticity in the Neural Representation of Actions and Objects
proposals.motivation: The organization of category-selective regions in the ventral and lateral temporal cortex (e.g., FFA, PPA, EBA) is often described as stable and innate. However, this static view is challenged by evidence of cortical plasticity following sensory loss or skill acquisition. It remains unknown whether high-level, category-specific representations for complex visual events, such as actions and object interactions, retain a similar capacity for large-scale reorganization following focal damage or intensive training, which is critical for understanding the principles of neural resilience and recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that category-selective regions for actions and manipulable objects (e.g., in lateral occipito-temporal cortex) are not statically fixed but can be dynamically recruited or reorganized following focal cortical disruption (e.g., TMS) or through intensive perceptual-motor training, with the new neural code preserving representational geometry related to physical and agentive event properties.
proposals.method: We will combine fMRI-based multivoxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA) with targeted interventions. In a lesion-model study, we will use repetitive TMS to transiently disrupt the right extrastriate body area (EBA) in healthy participants, measuring pre- and post-intervention fMRI responses to action and object event videos. In a complementary training study, participants will undergo intensive discrimination training on novel, physically defined action categories. We will test for changes in neural selectivity and the alignment of neural patterns with a computational model of event physics, building on Caramazza's prior work on a shared neural code for action physics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct evidence for or against the fixed organization of high-level visual category areas. It will elucidate the principles governing cortical plasticity for event representations, bridging the gap between studies of low-level plasticity and high-level cognitive neuroscience. The findings will have implications for neurorehabilitation strategies targeting semantic and event knowledge deficits.
proposals.novelty: While plasticity is well-documented in early sensory cortex, testing the reorganization of high-level, abstract category representations—specifically for dynamic events defined by physical and agentive forces—is a novel and critical extension. The approach uniquely integrates causal disruption (TMS) with longitudinal training and a model-based RSA framework to track representational content during reorganization.

proposals.title: Neural Dissociation of Animate and Inanimate Categories in the Intact Brain: An fMRI Investigation of Representational Geometry and Event-Related Responses
proposals.motivation: Category-specific semantic deficits in brain-damaged patients, such as selective impairments for animals, have long suggested domain-specific neural organization. However, it remains debated whether these findings reflect fundamental, hardwired distinctions in the healthy brain or are epiphenomena of damage to distributed networks. Resolving this debate is central to theories of conceptual representation and object recognition. Pre-2026 neuroimaging in healthy individuals has yielded mixed results, often failing to find clear, replicable category-specific activations for animals versus tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the intact human brain contains dissociable neural representations for animate (animal) and inanimate (tool) categories, but that these distinctions are best revealed not by univariate activation contrasts, but by multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) of representational geometry and by examining responses to dynamic, action-related events typical of each category.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution (3T) fMRI to scan healthy participants while they view static images and short video clips of animals and manipulable tools. The design incorporates both passive viewing and an active property verification task. We will employ a multi-faceted analytical approach: 1) Univariate GLM analysis to identify regions with category-preferential responses (e.g., lateral occipital cortex, posterior fusiform). 2) MVPA (using a linear support vector machine classifier) to test for decodable category information within these regions and across the ventral temporal cortex. 3) Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) to compare the neural representational geometries for animals and tools, testing if they align with models based on visual shape, semantic features, and action affordances (e.g., grip-related information).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive test of category-specific organization in the neurotypical brain using state-of-the-art pre-2026 multivariate methods. It will bridge Caramazza's work on category-specific deficits with his recent focus on the neural representation of actions and events, clarifying whether the forces (agentive vs. physical) defining event perception are rooted in stable category distinctions. The findings will constrain cognitive and neural models of the ventral visual stream and semantic memory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond simple picture-viewing paradigms to integrate dynamic event stimuli and action-related property judgments, thereby probing the conceptual and event-based dimensions that may underpin category distinctions. Furthermore, applying RSA with models derived from both visual and action-based theories offers a direct test of competing accounts of category organization.

proposals.title: Developmental Trajectories of Category-Specific Neural Organization for Objects and Actions: An fMRI Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including work from Caramazza's lab, has established that the adult ventral and lateral occipitotemporal cortex exhibit a stable, categorical organization for objects (e.g., tools, animals) and actions. The origins of this organization are unknown: is it an intrinsic property of the developing brain, or does it emerge gradually through extensive perceptual and linguistic experience? Resolving this debate is critical for understanding the interplay between innate neural architecture and experience in shaping high-level cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the broad categorical distinction between animate and inanimate entities, and between agentive and physical motion events, will be present in early childhood, but that the fine-grained, domain-specific organization for categories like tools will become more pronounced and functionally segregated with age and increasing experience.
proposals.method: We will use functional MRI to measure neural responses in children (ages 5-7, 8-10) and adults while they perform passive viewing and one-back matching tasks. Stimuli will include images and short videos from categories central to Caramazza's work: manipulable tools, non-manipulable artifacts, animals, and scenes, as well as videos depicting agentive (hand actions) and physical motion events. We will employ multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode category information within predefined regions of interest (lateral occipital complex, posterior middle temporal gyrus, parahippocampal place area). Representational similarity analysis (RSA) will be used to compare neural representational geometries across age groups.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence for the developmental timeline of category-specific neural organization in the human brain. It will clarify whether foundational categorical divisions are pre-wired or learned, directly informing theories of cognitive and neural modularity. The findings will bridge developmental cognitive neuroscience with adult neuropsychology and computational models of category learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior developmental fMRI studies have examined broad domain responses, none have directly tested for the emergence of the specific, fine-grained categorical organization for objects and actions documented in adults by Caramazza and others, nor have they explicitly linked the development of object and event representations.

proposals.title: A Connectionist Model of Category-Specific Deficits: Lesioning Input Domains to Simulate the Neural Representation of Objects and Events
proposals.motivation: Category-specific semantic deficits (e.g., impaired recognition of living things vs. tools) have been pivotal in understanding the organization of conceptual knowledge. Pre-2026 theories, including the Sensory/Functional Theory and the Domain-Specific Hypothesis, often debate whether these deficits arise from damage to modality-specific (e.g., visual vs. action) or domain-specific (e.g., animate vs. inanimate) neural systems. Computational models that directly test these competing accounts by simulating lesions to different input streams are needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that category-specific deficits for living things and tools can be simulated in a connectionist model by selectively lesioning distinct input domains (visual-form vs. action/motor features), and that the pattern of deficits will align with the Domain-Specific Hypothesis, showing a stronger dependence of tool knowledge on action-related input.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-layer neural network model with separate, structured input layers representing distinct feature domains: visual-form (shape, texture) and action/motor (manipulation, functional motion). These will project to a convergent hidden layer representing integrated conceptual knowledge, which then projects to a name output layer. After training the model to associate input feature patterns with object and event names, we will systematically lesion connections from specific input domains (e.g., silencing the action stream) and evaluate the resulting category-specific performance profiles, comparing them to neuropsychological data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic model of how damage to different input streams produces dissociable category deficits, directly testing competing neuropsychological theories. It will bridge Caramazza's work on the neural representation of objects and events with connectionist modeling, offering a principled explanation for why certain categories (tools/actions) might be more vulnerable to damage in lateral occipito-temporal or fronto-parietal cortex.
proposals.novelty: While past models have lesioned distributed semantic representations, this model uniquely incorporates and lesions structured, theoretically motivated input domains (visual vs. action) aligned with pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience, directly simulating the causal impact of damage to different information types on category-specific performance.

proposals.title: Causal Dissociation of Agentive and Physical Event Representations in the Left Temporal Cortex: A TMS Investigation
proposals.motivation: Neuropsychological studies of patients with left temporal lesions suggest category-specific deficits for processing events, particularly those involving agents and actions versus physical, non-biological forces. However, lesion studies cannot establish the causal necessity of specific cortical regions for these distinct event types in the intact brain. This gap limits our understanding of the neural architecture for conceptual knowledge of events, a core component of language and visual cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transient disruption of the left posterior middle temporal gyrus (pMTG) using TMS will selectively impair the conceptual processing of agentive motion events (e.g., 'the man kicks'), while sparing or affecting differently the processing of physical motion events (e.g., 'the leaf falls'), thereby causally dissociating their neural substrates.
proposals.method: We will employ neuronavigated, repetitive TMS (rTMS) to temporarily disrupt the left pMTG, a region implicated in action and event representation. In a within-subjects design, participants will perform a semantic similarity judgment task on visually presented event sentences and matched videos. The task will contrast agentive and physical motion events. TMS will be applied in separate sessions targeting the left pMTG and a control site (vertex). Behavioral performance (accuracy and reaction time) will be compared across conditions and stimulation sites to isolate a reversible, category-specific deficit.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence for a functional dissociation within the left temporal cortex for different conceptual event categories. It will directly test predictions from Caramazza's theoretical framework on the role of agentive versus physical forces. The findings will bridge patient-based neuropsychology and functional imaging, offering a mechanistic account of how event knowledge is organized in the healthy brain.
proposals.novelty: While TMS has been used to study object categories, its application to causally dissociate conceptual event categories based on force dynamics (agentive vs. physical) is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational fMRI evidence to test the necessity of a shared neural code for action physics in a specific cortical region.

proposals.title: Decoding the Neural Representation of Event Semantics: An MVPA Investigation of Agentive vs. Physical Forces in Lateral Occipitotemporal Cortex
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in cognitive neuroscience is understanding how the brain represents the semantic structure of events, which integrates objects, actions, and their causal relations. Pre-2026 work by Caramazza and colleagues has identified distinct neural substrates for processing agentive (e.g., a person kicking) versus physical (e.g., a ball rolling) forces in motion events. However, the precise, distributed neural patterns that code for these distinct semantic event categories remain poorly characterized at a fine-grained level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate activity patterns in lateral occipitotemporal cortex (LOTC), a region implicated in both action and object processing, will contain decodable information that distinguishes between events defined by agentive forces and those defined by physical forces, even when controlling for low-level visual and kinematic features.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and new fMRI data from participants viewing videos of agentive and physical motion events. Using a whole-brain searchlight approach with multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), we will train linear support vector machine (SVM) classifiers to discriminate between event categories based on local BOLD activity patterns. We will complement this with region-of-interest (ROI) analyses focused on LOTC, employing regularized logistic regression to identify the most informative voxels for the classification. All analyses will control for visual motion and object form.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a high-resolution, pattern-based map of how fundamental semantic forces in events are neurally encoded, directly testing theoretical claims from Caramazza's recent work. It will bridge the gap between univariate localization findings and a mechanistic understanding of distributed semantic representation, offering a model for how event concepts are built from integrated action and object information.
proposals.novelty: While MVPA has been used to decode objects and actions separately, applying it to decode the *integrative semantic dimension* of causal force (agentive vs. physical) within event representations is a novel and critical step. This moves beyond mapping constituent parts to decoding the relational structure that defines an event, a central goal in pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience.

proposals.title: A High-Density Semantic Space: Mapping the Large-Scale Neural Architecture of Conceptual Knowledge with fMRI and Representational Similarity Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on the neural organization of semantic memory has largely focused on a limited set of categories (e.g., tools, animals, faces). This leaves the full organizational structure of semantic knowledge—encompassing abstract concepts, events, and complex object properties—poorly mapped. A systematic, large-scale investigation is needed to test competing theories of semantic representation, such as domain-specific, modality-specific, or distributed hub-and-spoke models, and to understand how diverse conceptual dimensions are integrated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural organization of semantic memory in the lateral and ventral temporal cortex follows a continuous, high-dimensional representational space, where the similarity between neural activity patterns for concepts is predicted by a combination of their sensorimotor features, event-related dynamics, and agentive properties, rather than by discrete category boundaries.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to measure neural responses in participants viewing and reading about items from a systematically constructed battery of >50 semantic categories, spanning concrete objects, actions, events, and abstract concepts. Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) will be the core analytical framework. We will construct multiple computational models representing competing organizational principles (e.g., visual shape, action affordances, thematic associations, linguistic distributional semantics from pre-trained word2vec or GloVe models) and test their ability to predict the neural representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) across the brain, with a focus on the lateral occipital complex, posterior middle temporal gyrus, and angular gyrus.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, model-driven map of the neural organization for a broad spectrum of semantic knowledge. It will directly test and quantify the contributions of different feature dimensions (visual, action, event-structural) to semantic representations, moving beyond binary category distinctions. The resulting high-density semantic space will serve as a foundational resource for theories of conceptual representation and their breakdown in neurological disorders.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have compared a handful of categories, the systematic scale and the direct modeling of a multi-dimensional feature space integrating object properties, action physics, and event structure—core themes of Caramazza's recent work—is novel. This approach moves from asking *where* categories are represented to explaining *why* the neural geometry of concepts takes its specific form.

proposals.title: Residual Cross-Modal Integration in Semantic Cognition: Probing the Interdependence of Action and Object Knowledge in Patient Lesion Models
proposals.motivation: Theoretical models of semantic memory, such as the Sensory/Functional Theory and Domain-Specific Hypothesis, often posit functionally independent neural systems for different semantic categories (e.g., tools vs. animals). However, recent work from Caramazza's lab demonstrates a shared neural code for the physics of actions and object events, suggesting potential cross-talk. It remains unknown whether this reflects deep interdependence or parallel, independent computations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that semantic systems for objects and actions are not strictly independent; even in patients with selective deficits for one domain (e.g., action semantics), residual, implicit integration of information from the 'impaired' domain will facilitate processing in the 'intact' domain.
proposals.method: We will test patients with focal lesions and documented dissociations (e.g., impaired tool use/action knowledge but preserved object recognition, and vice-versa) using a priming paradigm with fMRI. Participants will judge object identity or action feasibility. Critically, cross-modal primes (e.g., an action video preceding a tool object decision) will be used. We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses to test for above-chance classification of object categories from 'action' cortex activity in object-impaired patients, and to identify residual functional connectivity between lateral occipitotemporal cortex (object form) and left ventral premotor cortex (action) across patient groups.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first direct neuropsychological evidence for obligatory cross-modal integration in semantic memory, challenging strong modularity views. It will refine models of semantic organization by specifying the conditions and neural pathways through which different semantic stores interact, with implications for neurorehabilitation strategies.
proposals.novelty: While patient dissociations are classic evidence for independence, systematically probing for residual integration in these same patients using sensitive cross-modal priming and connectivity analyses is a novel, strong test of system interdependence that moves beyond simple double dissociations.

proposals.title: Modality-Specific Semantic Access: Dissociating Visual and Haptic Inputs to Object Knowledge Using fMRI and Representational Similarity Analysis
proposals.motivation: A central debate in cognitive neuroscience concerns the format of conceptual knowledge. While Caramazza's work on the neural representation of events and objects suggests modality-specific contributions, many models assume a single, amodal semantic hub. Nonverbal tasks (e.g., matching, property verification) are often used to probe this system, but it remains untested whether such tasks using different sensory inputs (e.g., vision vs. touch) necessarily access the same underlying semantic representations or recruit distinct, modality-biased systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that nonverbal tasks probing object knowledge will engage dissociable neural patterns depending on input modality, with visual inputs preferentially activating lateral occipital complex (LOC) and ventral temporal regions, while haptic inputs will preferentially engage parietal somatosensory areas and a distinct subset of temporal regions, challenging the notion of a unitary 'visual semantic' system accessed by all nonverbal tasks.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to scan participants while they perform a shape-matching task with the same set of objects under two conditions: visual (viewing images) and haptic (exploring 3D-printed objects blindfolded). Representational Similarity Analysis (RSA) will be employed as the core analytical framework. We will construct model representational dissimilarity matrices (RDMs) for visual shape (from a pre-trained convolutional neural network like VGG-19), haptic shape (from 3D mesh models), and conceptual categories. We will test where in the cortex the neural RDMs correlate with each model RDM, specifically testing for dissociations between LOC and anterior intraparietal sulcus.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct evidence for or against the modality-specificity of semantic access routes, refining models of the conceptual system. It will clarify the functional roles of the ventral and dorsal visual streams in semantic processing. The findings will have implications for understanding knowledge deficits in patients, suggesting that impairments may be tied to specific input modalities rather than a global semantic loss.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have compared visual and haptic shape perception, this proposal uniquely tests whether the *semantic access* pathway differs by modality within a unified RSA framework, directly targeting a core assumption in the literature on nonverbal semantic tasks. It leverages Caramazza's recent focus on action/event physics to inform the selection of manipulable objects, allowing us to test interactions between agentive/functional knowledge and input modality.

proposals.title: Dissociating Fine-Grained Semantic Access from Lexical Retrieval: A Multimodal Investigation of Visual and Right Hemisphere Contributions to Nonverbal Event Knowledge
proposals.motivation: Research on semantic memory and category-specific deficits has traditionally relied on verbal tasks (e.g., naming, word-picture matching), which conflate conceptual access with lexical retrieval. This limits our understanding of the neural substrates of high-level, nonverbal semantic knowledge, particularly for dynamic events and object interactions. Developing detailed nonverbal tasks is crucial to test theories of right hemisphere contributions to semantics and to characterize the nature of spared visual semantic processing in patients with left hemisphere language deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the right hemisphere and ventral visual stream support fine-grained, modality-specific semantic access to event and object properties, independent of the left-lateralized lexical retrieval system. This semantic access will be measurable through precise, nonverbal behavioral and neural responses.
proposals.method: We will design a novel battery of computerized, forced-choice matching tasks that require fine-grained discriminations within semantic categories (e.g., matching tools to typical recipient objects based on precise functional affordances, or matching agents to typical actions based on kinematic properties). These tasks will be administered to neurotypical participants and patients (e.g., with left temporal lobe lesions and anomia) during fMRI. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to decode semantic information from neural activity in regions of interest (lateral occipital complex, posterior middle temporal gyrus, right homologues) and psychophysical methods to titrate task difficulty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new methodological framework for isolating nonverbal semantic access, moving beyond coarse category judgments. It will directly test the neural code for action and event semantics, bridging Caramazza's work on object/event representation with questions of hemispheric specialization. The findings will clarify the functional architecture of the semantic system, with implications for neuropsychological assessment and rehabilitation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has used nonverbal tasks, they often test broad category knowledge. Our approach innovates by requiring fine-grained, within-category semantic judgments about events and object interactions, providing a more sensitive probe of high-level visual semantics and its dissociation from language.

proposals.title: A Shared Neural Code for Object Affordances and Event Roles Revealed by Non-Verbal Matching-to-Sample Tasks
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work, shows that the brain represents actions and object events in a shared neural code, integrating agentive and physical forces. However, the extent to which this code supports purely conceptual, non-linguistic knowledge of object function and event participation remains unclear. This gap is critical for understanding the interface between visual object recognition and event comprehension, independent of language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the lateral occipital complex (LOC) and posterior middle temporal gyrus (pMTG) will show graded fMRI adaptation effects during a non-verbal matching task, reflecting the semantic relatedness of objects based on their functional affordances and thematic event roles, not just visual similarity.
proposals.method: We will adapt animal cognition matching-to-sample paradigms for fMRI. On each trial, a target object (e.g., 'hammer') is presented, followed by two choice objects varying in semantic relatedness (e.g., 'nail' vs. 'banana'). Participants select the matching object via button press. We will manipulate relatedness along dimensions of functional affordance (hammer-nail) and event role (hammer-carpenter). fMRI analyses will employ multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and representational similarity analysis (RSA) to test for shared neural patterns between object pairs, controlling for visual features using HMAX model outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide direct evidence for a non-linguistic, conceptual neural code that bridges object recognition and event knowledge. It will specify how the brain organizes object concepts based on action-relevant properties and event participation, advancing models of semantic memory. The findings will constrain theories of how conceptual knowledge emerges from the integration of visual and action information.
proposals.novelty: While matching tasks are common in animal and infant research, applying them with modern fMRI analysis (MVPA/RSA) to dissociate conceptual from visual similarity in adults is novel. This directly tests Caramazza's 'shared neural code' hypothesis at a purely conceptual level, moving beyond verb-argument or motion event paradigms.

proposals.title: Dissociating Visual Form and Conceptual Access: A Neurocomputational Model of Intact Naming with Impaired Object Recognition
