proposals.motivation: Classical neuropsychological models posit that visual object recognition is a necessary precursor for naming. However, rare case reports (e.g., patients with visual agnosia who can name objects) challenge this serial view, suggesting a potential dissociation between deriving a stable visual representation and accessing its associated conceptual/lexical knowledge. Investigating this 'inverse' dissociation is critical for understanding the architecture of the ventral visual stream and its interaction with language systems, moving beyond the more commonly studied associative agnosia.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific computational lesion to intermediate shape-centered representations, sparing both early visual features and later amodal conceptual representations, can selectively impair visual object recognition while preserving the ability to name the same objects from vision.
proposals.method: We will develop a deep neural network model of the visual-lexical pathway, structured to reflect known neuroanatomy. The model will incorporate a pre-trained vision transformer (ViT) as an early feature hierarchy, a trainable intermediate module simulating the Lateral Occipital Complex (LOC) for shape-centered representation, and a semantic/lexical output layer. Using targeted noise injection and representational lesioning techniques (e.g., disrupting specific layers or connectivity patterns), we will simulate the hypothesized deficit. The model's behavior will be validated against human psychophysical and neuroimaging data (fMRI patterns in LOC) from control participants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit computational account of the intact-naming/poor-recognition dissociation, specifying the representational and informational constraints that make it possible. It will generate testable predictions about the neural locus of the deficit and the conditions under which conceptual access can bypass a degraded structural description, refining the theoretical framework for object and event representation championed by Caramazza's lab.
proposals.novelty: While most models focus on simulating recognition failures (e.g., agnosia), modeling the preservation of naming despite recognition failure is a novel, counter-intuitive approach. It directly tests the necessity of intermediate visual representations for lexical access, a core but untested assumption in many theories.

proposals.title: Neural Reorganization of Action and Object Semantic Systems Following Ventral Stream Damage: An fMRI and Behavioral Study of Rehabilitation-Induced Reintegration
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Caramazza and others has established that semantic knowledge for objects and actions is supported by distinct, large-scale neural systems in the ventral and lateral temporal cortex. Following brain damage (e.g., stroke), these systems can become functionally disconnected, leading to specific semantic deficits. However, the mechanisms by which these systems might reintegrate or functionally compensate during successful rehabilitation remain poorly understood, limiting the development of targeted therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful rehabilitation of object and action semantics after ventral stream damage will be associated with (1) increased functional connectivity between spared regions of the lateral occipitotemporal cortex (action system) and anterior temporal lobes (object system), and (2) the recruitment of domain-general frontal regions to mediate cross-system information exchange.
proposals.method: We will study patients with semantic deficits following left ventral temporal stroke longitudinally through a 6-month rehabilitation protocol. The protocol uses a computerized training paradigm that forces the integration of object and action semantics (e.g., matching tools to their typical actions). We will employ pre- and post-rehabilitation fMRI using multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) to track changes in neural representations for objects and actions. Functional connectivity (psychophysiological interaction, PPI) analyses will test for increased coupling between the lateral occipitotemporal cortex (e.g., MTG) and anterior temporal lobe. We will correlate neural changes with detailed behavioral measures of semantic integration.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal neural evidence for how disconnected semantic systems reorganize during recovery. It will identify specific neural pathways (lateral-frontal) that support semantic reintegration, moving beyond static lesion-deficit models. The findings will directly inform the design of rehabilitation protocols that target neural network plasticity rather than isolated cognitive functions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has mapped the dissociable systems, this proposal uniquely investigates their dynamic *re-integration* in the damaged brain. It combines Caramazza's recent focus on the shared neural code for actions and object events with a novel rehabilitation neuroscience approach to study plasticity within high-level semantic networks.

proposals.title: Neural Substrates of Cognate Facilitation: An fMRI Investigation of Shared Lexical-Conceptual Representations in Bilingual Production
proposals.motivation: The cognate facilitation effect, where bilinguals produce words with overlapping form and meaning faster and more accurately, is a key behavioral marker for shared lexical-conceptual representations. However, its neural basis remains poorly understood. Grounded in Caramazza's work on object and event representation, this study aims to bridge language processing with visual cognition by identifying the cortical regions where shared semantic and phonological information reduces neural processing load.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the production of cognates, compared to non-cognate translation equivalents, will elicit reduced activation in a left-lateralized network encompassing the left inferior frontal gyrus (LIFG) and the left posterior middle temporal gyrus (pMTG), reflecting more efficient retrieval due to convergent semantic and phonological information.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to scan highly proficient Spanish-English bilinguals during a picture-naming task. The design will include conditions for cognates, non-cognates, and baseline objects. We will employ a standard event-related GLM analysis to identify regions with significant BOLD signal reductions for cognates. Crucially, we will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to test whether the neural patterns in regions like the pMTG, implicated in conceptual event representation, show greater similarity between cognate pairs than non-cognate pairs, linking language-specific facilitation to domain-general conceptual hubs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct neuroimaging localization of the cognate facilitation effect in production, moving beyond behavioral and ERP evidence. It will critically inform models of the bilingual lexicon by specifying which cortical nodes in the language network benefit from form-meaning overlap. Furthermore, it will test the hypothesis that conceptual representations for objects and events, as studied in Caramazza's lab, interact with language-specific lexical form representations to drive processing efficiency.
proposals.novelty: While cognate effects are well-studied behaviorally, their neural signature in production is not mapped. The novelty lies in using fMRI and MVPA to test if facilitation arises from reduced demand on amodal conceptual regions (e.g., pMTG) in addition to classic language areas, directly integrating the study of bilingualism with research on the neural representation of objects and events.

proposals.title: Disentangling Linguistic and Sensorimotor Contributions to ERP Signatures of Event Representation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Caramazza's work on the neural representation of motion events, suggests a shared neural code for action physics and event perception. However, ERP studies linking specific components (e.g., N400, P600) to linguistic distinctions like agentive vs. physical forces are confounded by non-linguistic cognitive factors such as visual complexity, predictability, and sensorimotor simulation. It is critical to determine if these neural markers are truly linguistic or reflect more general event-processing mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early (~N400) ERP differences observed for linguistically distinct event categories (e.g., 'push' vs. 'fall') will be attenuated or eliminated when non-linguistic cognitive factors—specifically, visual predictability and implied physical dynamics—are rigorously controlled and matched across conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ a visual-world paradigm combined with high-density EEG. Participants will view short, controlled animations of object events (e.g., launching, breaking) while hearing sentences that vary linguistic agency (agentive vs. non-agentive verbs). Critically, we will use psychophysical scaling (pre-2026 methods) to precisely match animations for visual complexity and implied physical forces. ERP analysis will focus on the N400 and P600 components. We will use multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) on the EEG data to decode event categories from neural activity, testing whether decoding accuracy is driven by linguistic labels or low-level visual and physical features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a clearer functional interpretation of ERP components in event perception, directly testing their specificity to linguistic processing. It will advance theories of the brain's event representation system by delineating the contributions of language from core physical and action-based representations, a central theme in Caramazza's research program.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has noted ERP differences, this study is novel in its systematic, factorial manipulation and matching of linguistic and non-linguistic variables within a single paradigm, allowing for a direct test of their unique neural contributions, moving beyond correlation to causal inference.

proposals.title: Neural Dynamics of Morphosyntactic Processing: An ERP Investigation of Tense, Agreement, and Case Inflections
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 ERP research on inflectional morphology has predominantly focused on noun plurals and verb participles, revealing components like the N400 and P600. However, it remains unclear whether these neural signatures generalize to other core inflectional phenomena, such as tense marking, subject-verb agreement, and case marking, which involve distinct syntactic and semantic computations. Extending this work is critical for developing a comprehensive, neurally-grounded model of how the brain constructs grammatical meaning from form.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that different classes of inflectional morphology (tense, agreement, case) will elicit distinct spatiotemporal patterns of neural activity, as measured by ERPs, reflecting their differential reliance on syntactic unification processes versus lexical-semantic retrieval. Specifically, we predict that agreement violations will robustly engage a left-anterior negativity (LAN) and P600, while tense errors may modulate N400 effects due to their semantic-temporal implications.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density (128-channel) EEG to record ERPs while participants perform a grammaticality judgment task on visually presented sentences in Italian and German, languages rich in the target inflections. Critical stimuli will include violations of tense (e.g., *yesterday he walks*), agreement (e.g., *the keys is*), and case (e.g., German *mit den Mann*). Data analysis will involve standard pre-processing pipelines, followed by time-frequency analysis and source localization using pre-2026 standard algorithms (e.g., sLORETA) to identify the cortical generators of observed effects, linking them to known language networks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first systematic ERP characterization of a broad range of inflectional types, directly testing the functional specificity of neural components like the LAN and N400. It will contribute essential data to theories of the neural representation of grammatical knowledge, bridging Caramazza's work on conceptual representation with the dynamics of syntactic processing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work often treated inflection as a monolithic category, this proposal systematically dissects it into functionally distinct sub-types (tense, agreement, case) for direct neural comparison, moving beyond the well-studied domains of plurality and participles to a more complete typology of morphosyntax.

proposals.title: A Connectionist Model of Inflectional Morphology: Linking Visual Object Recognition to Linguistic Event Structure
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in cognitive neuroscience, including Caramazza's work, has identified a shared neural code for representing the physics of actions and object events. This suggests that the cognitive systems for processing visual events and linguistic events may be deeply intertwined. However, the computational mechanisms by which these shared representations support the acquisition of grammatical structure, such as inflection, remain poorly understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a connectionist model, trained on the mapping from object/event representations to inflected word forms, will reveal that the statistical regularities of visual events provide a crucial inductive bias for learning linguistic inflection. Specifically, we predict the model will mirror neuropsychological dissociations (e.g., regular vs. irregular inflection) based on the event-structural properties of its inputs.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-layer neural network model. The input layer will encode structured representations of events, drawing on pre-2026 models of visual event perception (e.g., agentive/physical forces, object affordances) to create feature vectors. The output layer will represent inflected phonological forms. We will train the model using backpropagation through time (BPTT) on a corpus of verb-event pairings, systematically varying event properties. Model performance and internal representations will be analyzed for generalization, error patterns, and compared to human behavioral data from priming and inflection tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, process-based account of how non-linguistic event cognition scaffolds language acquisition. It will generate testable predictions for neural representation, linking activity in lateral temporal cortex (for event structure) to language areas. The model will serve as a formal bridge between Caramazza's research on object/event representation and theories of grammatical processing.
proposals.novelty: While connectionist models of inflection exist, none have grounded the training input in computationally explicit, psychologically plausible representations of visual events and physical actions derived from cognitive neuroscience. This directly tests the functional relevance of a shared neural code for actions and events in a core linguistic domain.

proposals.title: Neural Dissociations in Morphological Processing: A Cognitive Neuropsychological Study of Regular and Irregular Inflection in Aphasia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience debates center on whether distinct neural systems support the processing of regular versus irregular morphological forms (e.g., 'walked' vs. 'ran'). This distinction bears directly on broader theories of the neural representation of events and actions, a core theme in Caramazza's work, as it may reflect a fundamental division between combinatorial rule-based processes and lexical-associative memory. Testing this in patient populations with focal brain lesions provides a critical causal test of neurocognitive models derived from neuroimaging and computational studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients with left inferior frontal and/or anterior temporal lobe damage will show a double dissociation: selective deficits for producing/processing regular inflected forms versus irregular forms, respectively. This would support the claim that regular inflection relies on a combinatorial neural circuit distinct from the lexical-semantic network supporting irregulars.
proposals.method: We will conduct detailed case-series studies with carefully selected individuals with non-fluent and fluent aphasia (e.g., Broca's, Wernicke's, Anomic). Tasks will include spoken and written production, comprehension, and grammaticality judgment of regular and irregular past tense verbs and plural nouns, controlling for frequency, phonological complexity, and imageability. Performance will be analyzed using established methods from cognitive neuropsychology (e.g., dissociations in accuracy and reaction time) and lesion-symptom mapping (using pre-2026 standard MRI/CT lesion overlay and voxel-based methods) to correlate specific deficit patterns with lesion loci.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide causal neuropsychological evidence for or against the neural separability of combinatorial morphological rules. It will directly inform models of the language system's architecture and its interface with conceptual knowledge of actions and events. The findings will also refine our understanding of the functional roles of specific left hemisphere regions (e.g., BA 44/45 vs. temporal pole) in linguistic computation.
proposals.novelty: While past patient studies exist, this proposal leverages Caramazza's precise theoretical framework on event representation and his group's expertise in detailed single-case methodology to test a specific neural hypothesis linking grammatical processing to broader action/event cognition, moving beyond purely linguistic accounts.

proposals.title: Decoding the Processing Route: Using Multivariate Pattern Analysis to Distinguish Ventral and Dorsal Stream Engagement from fMRI Activity
proposals.motivation: A central tenet of visual neuroscience is the dual-stream hypothesis, with the ventral stream supporting object recognition and the dorsal stream supporting action and spatial analysis. However, it remains a challenge to determine, from brain activity alone, which processing route is predominantly engaged during a given cognitive task, especially for stimuli that could be processed by either stream. This inverse problem is crucial for understanding the flexible routing of visual information.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that multivariate patterns of fMRI activity, particularly in intermediate and higher-tier visual areas (e.g., lateral occipital complex, posterior parietal cortex), contain sufficient information to reliably decode whether a participant is engaging a shape-centered (ventral) or action/spatial (dorsal) processing route, even for identical visual stimuli.
proposals.method: We will use fMRI to record brain activity while participants perform two tasks on the same set of visual stimuli (e.g., tools, graspable objects): a recognition task (ventral route) and an action-judgment task (dorsal route). We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA), specifically linear support vector machine (SVM) classifiers, to decode the task condition from activity patterns within predefined regions of interest along the ventral and dorsal streams. We will also use representational similarity analysis (RSA) to compare neural representational geometries to models of shape-based vs. action-based processing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, data-driven method for identifying the active processing pathway from neural signals, moving beyond traditional lesion or task-contrast approaches. It will test the specificity of route-related neural codes and inform theories of interactive versus parallel processing between the streams, as suggested by Caramazza's work on shared codes for actions and object events.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies decode object categories or actions, decoding the *processing route* itself from activity patterns is a novel inverse problem. It leverages the distinct neural codes for object shape versus action/spatial properties identified in prior work to infer the cognitive pathway being utilized.

proposals.title: Task-Demand Artifacts in Category-Specific Deficits: A Computational Challenge to the Modularity of Semantic Memory
proposals.motivation: Neuropsychological dissociations, such as category-specific deficits for living vs. non-living things, have been foundational evidence for modular semantic architectures. However, pre-2026 work in cognitive neuroscience increasingly highlights the role of distributed, interactive systems and the influence of non-semantic factors (e.g., visual complexity, task demands) on performance. It remains critical to rigorously test whether observed dissociations truly reflect architectural divisions or emerge from performance artifacts in a damaged, interactive system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that category-specific dissociations can be fully simulated in a single, unitary neural network model of semantic knowledge by selectively degrading its input or output pathways, without requiring pre-existing modular structure dedicated to semantic categories.
proposals.method: We will implement a deep convolutional neural network (CNN) trained on a large dataset of object images (e.g., ImageNet) to perform both visual recognition and action/function naming tasks, integrating visual and functional semantic features. Using lesioning techniques (e.g., targeted noise injection, connection pruning) inspired by Caramazza's previous work on lateral occipital cortex and action information, we will selectively impair the model's early visual processing stream or its later visuo-motor integration pathways. We will then analyze the resulting error patterns across object categories, quantifying performance dissociations as a function of the lesion site and the specific task demands (e.g., naming vs. gesture selection).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete computational proof-of-concept that category-specific dissociations are not conclusive evidence for innate semantic modules. It will shift the theoretical debate by offering a rigorous, model-based alternative explanation grounded in the interaction of task demands, stimulus properties, and locus of damage within a distributed system. It directly challenges a core assumption in neuropsychology and refines the interpretation of patient data.
proposals.novelty: While previous models have shown category effects emerge from statistical learning, this proposal uniquely focuses on simulating precise neuropsychological *dissociations* (double dissociations) through strategic damage to a *non-modular* system engaged in both visual and action-based tasks, directly linking to Caramazza's recent work on the neural code for actions and object events.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Semantic Space: A Factor Analytic Approach to Agentive, Physical, and Object-Based Semantic Dimensions
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience debates the architecture of semantic memory, particularly whether it is a unitary system or composed of dissociable subsystems (e.g., for objects, actions, events). Caramazza's recent work on the neural representation of motion events highlights distinct roles for agentive and physical forces, suggesting separable semantic dimensions. However, a comprehensive, data-driven behavioral model of these dimensions is lacking. This project aims to provide an empirical, bottom-up characterization of the latent semantic factors underlying conceptual knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that factor analysis of rich behavioral data from diverse semantic tasks will reveal a multi-dimensional semantic space, with statistically separable factors corresponding to agentive/action-related, physical/mechanical, and object-specific (e.g., shape-based) semantic features.
proposals.method: We will collect behavioral data (e.g., feature generation, similarity judgments, property verification) for a broad set of nouns and verbs encompassing manipulable objects, non-manipulable objects, and action events. We will apply exploratory factor analysis (EFA) using principal axis factoring with oblique rotation (Promax) to the resulting feature-by-concept matrices to identify latent factors. Confirmatory factor analysis (CFA) will then test the fit of competing structural models (unitary vs. multi-factor) derived from the EFA and from theoretical frameworks like the Sensory/Functional Theory and Caramazza's work on action and event representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, quantitative model of the major dimensions of the semantic space, moving beyond dichotomous debates. It will generate testable predictions for neuroimaging and neuropsychological studies by linking specific behavioral factors to candidate neural substrates (e.g., lateral occipitotemporal cortex for physical/mechanical properties, fronto-parietal regions for agentive actions).
proposals.novelty: While factor analysis has been used in semantics, its application is novel here for directly testing theoretical distinctions from cognitive neuroscience—specifically, the separability of agentive and physical forces in conceptual representation—using a task battery designed to probe these specific dimensions.

proposals.title: Integrating the O.U.C.H. Model into a Unified Architecture for Perception, Memory, and Language
proposals.motivation: The Object Understanding through the Composition of Handles (O.U.C.H.) model provides a neurally plausible account of how object shape and action affordances are represented in the lateral occipitotemporal cortex. However, a critical gap remains in understanding how these intermediate visual representations interface with long-term memory systems and language comprehension to support the full cognitive processing of events. Bridging these domains is essential for a complete theory of how we perceive, remember, and talk about objects and actions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the shape- and action-based representations of the O.U.C.H. model serve as a fundamental computational hub, directly interfacing with (a) medial temporal lobe structures for episodic memory encoding and (b) left-lateralized temporal and frontal regions for lexical-semantic and syntactic processing of event descriptions.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational cognitive architecture integrating the O.U.C.H. model with established pre-2026 models. We will use a neurobiologically constrained neural network where the O.U.C.H. layer connects to a hippocampal-entorhinal circuit for memory (inspired by complementary learning systems theory) and to a language network trained on distributional semantics (e.g., a word2vec or BERT-like encoder). The model will be tested using fMRI and behavioral priming paradigms, where participants view action events and later perform memory or language tasks. We will analyze model-predicted representational similarity matrices against neural data from ventral visual, lateral occipitotemporal, and language cortex.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first explicit computational model of how object-action representations drive both memory formation and language comprehension. It will generate testable predictions about shared and distinct neural codes across perception, memory, and language systems. The integrated architecture will offer a framework for interpreting neuropsychological deficits in patients with lesions at different points in this proposed processing stream.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has linked visual and conceptual representations, this proposal is novel in its specific focus on grounding memory and language processes in the action- and physics-based intermediate representations of the O.U.C.H. model, moving beyond static object categories to dynamic event understanding.

proposals.title: Inverting the Cognitive Map: A Computational Framework for Inferring Semantic Architecture from Behavioral Dissociations in Action and Object Processing
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuropsychology has long relied on behavioral dissociations (e.g., selective deficits in naming tools vs. animals) to argue for distinct, domain-specific semantic subsystems. However, the formal logic linking patterns of performance to underlying latent cognitive architecture remains underspecified and often relies on intuitive, qualitative arguments. A rigorous, computational formulation of this 'inverse problem' is needed to move from descriptive patterns to testable architectural models, particularly for complex domains like action and event semantics where Caramazza's work has identified shared and distinct neural codes.
proposals.hypothesis: The latent, high-dimensional representational geometry of semantic knowledge—specifically the organization of object, action, and event concepts—can be formally inferred by modeling the differential impact of simulated 'lesions' to candidate representational subspaces on behavioral performance profiles observed in neuropsychological patients and healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational framework using pre-2026 methods. First, we will construct a base neural network model where semantic representations emerge as population codes in a high-dimensional latent space, informed by fMRI-based representational similarity analyses (RSA) from studies on action and object events. Second, we will implement a formal 'inversion engine' using Bayesian model inversion and variational inference techniques (e.g., as used in dynamic causal modeling for fMRI) to treat observed behavioral dissociation patterns as data. This engine will evaluate competing architectural hypotheses (e.g., strictly modular vs. continuous gradient organizations) by finding the latent parameterization (e.g., lesion location and shape in the representational space) that best predicts the observed performance profile.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, quantitative toolkit for moving beyond classical dissociation logic, offering a stronger foundation for architectural claims in cognitive neuropsychology. It will generate specific, testable predictions about the representational structure of action and object semantics that can be validated with neural data. The framework will be directly applicable to ongoing debates on the nature of category-specificity and the integration of agentive and physical forces in event representation.
proposals.novelty: While neural network lesioning is established, the novelty lies in formally framing behavioral dissociation as an inverse problem to be solved with Bayesian inference, explicitly targeting the latent semantic architecture rather than just replicating deficits. This shifts the focus from validating a single model to systematically comparing a space of architectural hypotheses defined by pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience findings.

proposals.title: Neural Precursors of Moral Justification: A High-Temporal-Resolution Test of the Epiphenomenal Justification Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: Prominent models of moral judgment, such as Greene's dual-process theory, posit a causal role for conscious reasoning. However, alternative views, like Haidt's social intuitionist model, suggest moral reasoning is often a post-hoc rationalization of automatic, intuitive judgments. This debate mirrors foundational questions in cognitive neuroscience about the causal role of conscious awareness. Resolving whether moral justifications are causal or epiphenomenal is critical for understanding the architecture of social cognition and the nature of moral responsibility.
proposals.hypothesis: The neural correlates of a moral decision, localized to value- and emotion-related brain regions, will reliably precede the moment a subject can articulate a conscious moral justification. This temporal precedence will provide evidence that the reported justification is a post-hoc rationalization rather than a causal driver of the judgment.
proposals.method: We will employ high-density EEG and MEG to measure neural activity with millisecond precision while participants make moral judgments on dilemmas designed to pit emotional intuition against utilitarian reasoning (e.g., personal vs. impersonal dilemmas). The key temporal markers will be: (1) the onset of decision-related neural activity, identified using multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) on EEG/MEG signals from regions of interest (e.g., vmPFC, amygdala, TPJ) derived from fMRI localizers, and (2) the behavioral onset of justification, measured via a timed verbal report or a forced-choice justification selection task. We will adapt the logic of Libet-style readiness potential experiments to compare these latencies across hundreds of trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, temporally precise neural test of the epiphenomenal justification hypothesis in moral psychology. It will adjudicate between competing models of moral judgment by establishing the causal sequence of neural and conscious events. Furthermore, it will extend Caramazza's research on the neural representation of events and actions into the domain of social and moral reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While the debate on post-hoc moral reasoning is established, directly testing it with the high-temporal-resolution methods and causal timing logic from the consciousness literature is novel. This approach moves beyond correlational fMRI studies to assess the critical temporal dimension of causality, applying a classic neuroscience paradigm to a core problem in social cognition.

proposals.title: Neural and Representational Dissociation of Agentive Force from Outcome in Moral Dilemma Judgments
proposals.motivation: The Doctrine of Double Effect (DDE) is a key principle in moral philosophy and psychology, positing that harmful outcomes are more permissible if they are foreseen side-effects rather than intended means. However, pre-2026 behavioral work suggests judgments attributed to DDE may be confounded by other factors like causal proximity or physical contact. This project aims to isolate the neural and conceptual representations of agentive force from outcome representations to test if DDE is a primary operative principle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that neural representations of *agentive force* (the 'means' vs. 'side-effect' distinction) in high-level action-selective cortical regions (e.g., lateral occipito-temporal cortex) will be dissociable from representations of *outcome valence* and *physical event kinematics*, and that the strength of an individual's neural distinction of agentive force will predict their moral judgments, not merely the physical event structure.
proposals.method: We will employ fMRI adaptation (fMRI-A) and multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) within Caramazza's established action/event perception paradigm. Participants will view animated vignettes of moral dilemmas (e.g., trolley problems) systematically varying agentive force (intended means vs. foreseen side-effect), outcome, and physical action kinematics. We will test for adaptation release in action-selective cortex (e.g., LOTC, pSTS) when agentive force changes versus when only kinematics or outcome change. A subsequent behavioral moral judgment task will allow us to correlate neural pattern dissimilarity for agentive force with judgment profiles.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first neural evidence for whether the DDE distinction has a privileged representational basis in the human action perception system, or if it is an epiphenomenon of lower-level physical or causal representations. It will bridge Caramazza's research on the neural representation of agentive forces in motion events with the cognitive neuroscience of moral reasoning.
proposals.novelty: While prior fMRI studies have identified brain regions active during moral judgment, none have used neural adaptation within an action representation framework to isolate and measure the specific representational code for the 'means/side-effect' distinction central to DDE, separating it from confounding physical and outcome variables.

proposals.title: Mapping the Neural Code for Unconscious Moral Appraisal: A Functional Dissection Using High-Temporal-Resolution Reporter Assays and Targeted Neuromodulation
proposals.motivation: Research in moral psychology suggests rapid, automatic intuitions precede and guide moral judgments, yet the specific cognitive and neural mechanisms of this unconscious appraisal system remain poorly understood. This gap mirrors the historical challenge in immunology of mapping precise, unconscious detection pathways. A functional, causal model of this initial appraisal is needed to bridge theories of moral intuition with neural mechanisms of event perception and social cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that unconscious moral appraisal is implemented by a rapid, domain-specific neural code for 'agentive harm,' analogous to a physical force representation, and that this code is functionally dissociable from later conscious evaluation. Specifically, we predict that disrupting early nodes in this network (e.g., amygdala, anterior insula) will selectively abolish automatic physiological responses to moral violations without affecting the perception of the constituent actions or objects.
proposals.method: We adapt immunological paradigms: 1) 'Reporter Assays': Using visual stimuli depicting events with systematically manipulated features (agent intent, physical force, outcome), we measure very early event-related potentials (N1, P1) and startle reflex as high-temporal-resolution output signals of unconscious appraisal. 2) 'Cognitive Knockout': We employ neuronavigated, event-locked transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) to temporarily inhibit candidate regions (amygdala via its connectivity to temporal cortex, anterior insula, ventromedial prefrontal cortex) during stimulus presentation. The combined design tests the necessity of these regions for generating the early 'reporter' signal, establishing a causal sequence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a causal, mechanistic account of the earliest stage of moral judgment, moving beyond correlational neuroimaging. It will directly test whether moral appraisal leverages neural systems for representing physical forces and actions, as suggested by Caramazza's recent work. The findings will offer a novel functional architecture for moral cognition, detailing necessary components and their temporal dynamics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct methodological analogy from immunology to cognitive neuroscience, combining high-temporal-resolution 'reporter' measures with targeted neuromodulation to perform a functional dissection of an unconscious cognitive system. This approach moves beyond identifying brain regions 'involved' in morality to testing their necessary role in a specific, rapid processing stage.

proposals.title: A Dual-Route Developmental Model of Moral Cognition: Computational and Neural Mechanisms of Judgment-Justification Dissociation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in moral psychology, particularly dual-process theories, posits distinct intuitive and deliberative systems but lacks a formal developmental account of how these systems emerge and interact over time. This gap mirrors the historical understanding of reading before the establishment of the dual-route model, which precisely explained developmental trajectories and dissociations like phonological dyslexia. Understanding the developmental dynamics of moral cognition is crucial for explaining why individuals, especially children, often provide justifications that diverge from their initial moral judgments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that moral cognition develops via two computationally and neurally distinct routes—an early-maturing, affective route for rapid judgment (reliant on structures like the amygdala and ventromedial prefrontal cortex) and a later-maturing, deliberative route for explicit justification (reliant on lateral prefrontal and parietal cortices)—and that dissociations between judgment and justification arise from asynchronous development or differential recruitment of these routes.
proposals.method: We will develop a connectionist dual-route model, adapting the pre-2026 Triangle model of reading as an architectural analogy. The model will feature a 'visual-semantic/affective' route (mapping situational features directly to moral valence) and a 'phonological/deliberative' route (mapping features to verbal justifications via a symbolic rule system). We will train and test the model on developmental datasets of moral dilemma responses. Concurrently, we will use fMRI to measure neural activity in children and adults during moral judgment and justification tasks, focusing on the lateral occipitotemporal cortex (for action/event processing, per Caramazza's work) and prefrontal regions, to constrain and validate the model's predicted developmental dissociations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal computational model of moral development that explains judgment-justification dissociations, moving beyond descriptive dual-process theories. It will generate testable predictions about developmental disorders of social cognition and identify specific neural signatures of mature versus immature moral reasoning. The project will also demonstrate the utility of applying well-established cognitive models (from reading) to new, complex domains like social and moral reasoning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly modeling moral development through the lens of a well-understood cognitive architecture (the dual-route reading model), a non-obvious analogy that provides a precise framework for explaining developmental dissociations. Furthermore, it integrates Caramazza's research on the neural representation of actions and events as a core input representation for the moral system, linking physical and social cognition.

proposals.title: Network Dysregulation of Agentive and Sensory Systems Underlies Dissociation: A Multi-Modal Causal Connectivity Study
proposals.motivation: Dissociation, a disruption in the integration of consciousness, identity, and perception, remains poorly understood at a systems neuroscience level. Pre-2026 work, including Caramazza's research on the neural representation of agentive and physical forces in events, suggests that the brain constructs a coherent perceptual world by integrating sensory evidence with agentive predictions. A failure in this integration could be a core mechanism of dissociation, but current studies typically examine neural or physiological signals in isolation, missing the critical cross-system dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dissociative state is characterized by a specific, quantifiable dysregulation in the causal interactions within a network spanning brain regions for agentive control (e.g., lateral prefrontal cortex) and sensory processing, coupled with autonomic nervous system markers of arousal and orienting.
proposals.method: We will induce a controlled, transient dissociative state in healthy participants using a validated sensory conflict paradigm (e.g., mirror-induced visual drift) while concurrently recording high-density EEG, fMRI, electrodermal activity, heart rate variability, and pupillometry. We will apply Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) for fMRI and EEG to infer the directed effective connectivity between key nodes (e.g., intraparietal sulcus, premotor cortex, insula). We will integrate these neural connectivity estimates with peripheral physiological time-series using multi-level vector autoregressive models to identify the cross-system 'dysregulation signature' of the dissociative episode.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, multi-system model of dissociation, moving beyond regional localization to a network dysregulation account. It will directly test and extend theories linking agentive processing to conscious unity by identifying the specific informational pathways whose decoupling constitutes a dissociative state.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a systems ecology-inspired, multi-variable network analysis to the study of consciousness, explicitly modeling the causal interactions between central (agentive/sensory) and peripheral (autonomic) systems during a controlled perturbation, rather than studying correlates in single modalities.

proposals.title: Neural and Physiological Dynamics of Heuristic-to-Algorithmic Switching in Visual Event Understanding
proposals.motivation: The brain efficiently interprets complex visual events, but the neural mechanisms governing the switch from fast, heuristic processing to slow, algorithmic analysis remain poorly understood. Grounded in Caramazza's work on the neural representation of actions and object events, this proposal seeks to map the real-time physiological triggers of this cognitive switch, a core problem in cognitive neuroscience with implications for understanding reasoning and decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: Heuristic processing of visual events is the metabolically efficient default, characterized by a specific neural signature (e.g., reduced frontal theta/alpha power, stable pupil diameter), and that this default is disrupted by event properties violating physical or agentive expectations, triggering a detectable physiological cascade (e.g., conflict-related N2/N400, pupil dilation, heart rate deceleration) that recruits algorithmic processing.
proposals.method: We will use high-density EEG and synchronized pupillometry/cardiac monitoring while participants view video stimuli of object interaction events. Stimuli will systematically manipulate physical plausibility and agentive force (based on Caramazza's prior work) to induce heuristic failures. Time-frequency analysis of EEG will identify load and conflict signatures. Pupil dilation and heart rate variability will index autonomic arousal. We will apply multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) to EEG data to decode the moment of cognitive switch and link it to physiological markers using cross-correlation and regression models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first integrated neural and physiological signature of the heuristic-to-algorithmic switch, directly linking cognitive theory to biological substrates. It will extend Caramazza's model of event representation by identifying the dynamic control mechanisms that gate access to detailed physical and agentive analyses.
proposals.novelty: While dual-process theories are well-established, directly tracking the real-time physiological failure of a heuristic default and the triggering of algorithmic processing in the domain of visual event understanding is novel. The fusion of neural conflict signals with autonomic arousal metrics to predict a cognitive switch represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Dissociating Agentive and Physical Forces in Event Perception: A Multimodal fMRI and Pupillometry Study
proposals.motivation: Caramazza's recent work (pre-2026) posits a shared neural code for the physics of actions and object events, but also highlights distinct roles for agentive vs. physical forces in motion event representation. The precise neural and physiological signatures of this proposed dissociation remain unclear. This gap is critical because understanding how the brain parses events based on force dynamics is fundamental to models of visual cognition and language.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observing events driven by agentive forces (e.g., a hand pushing a box) will selectively engage the left-lateralized action observation network, while events driven by physical forces (e.g., a ball rolling down a hill) will preferentially recruit bilateral dorsal stream regions involved in physical inference, with pupillometry revealing distinct cognitive load profiles for each event type.
proposals.method: We will use high-resolution fMRI (3T) and simultaneous pupillometry. Participants will view short videos of events systematically varying in force type (agentive/physical) and outcome. fMRI analysis will employ multivariate pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) to test for distinct, stable neural patterns and functional connectivity between lateral occipitotemporal cortex and parietal regions. Pupillometry data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to index processing load, time-locked to force onset.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, multimodal evidence for a neural and physiological dissociation in processing agentive versus physical forces, a core theoretical distinction. It will refine models of event representation by linking high-level conceptual forces to specific low-level physiological markers and neural circuits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified regions involved in action and physics perception separately, the novel integration of fMRI multivariate patterns with real-time pupillometry to dissociate these forces within a unified event perception paradigm is unprecedented. This moves beyond localization to characterize the dynamic processing signatures of distinct event components.

ideas.title: Neural Signal Detection Theory for Dissociating Processing Modes
ideas.description: This research program integrates the mathematical rigor of signal detection theory (SDT) with high-precision, multi-modal psychophysiological monitoring (e.g., EEG, pupillometry, fMRI) to dissociate cognitive processing modes. By designing tasks that pit algorithmic against heuristic processing, we can model behavioral performance in terms of sensitivity (d') and bias (criterion). Crucially, we then use the neural and physiological data to identify the biological signatures of these SDT parameters, asking, for instance, if a shift toward heuristic processing correlates with a specific change in frontal theta power or pupil-linked arousal that predicts a more liberal response criterion. This approach moves beyond correlating brain activity with behavior to formally modeling how distinct neural systems give rise to the fundamental signal detection properties that define different cognitive strategies.

ideas.title: Data-driven longitudinal phenotyping of cognitive and neural trajectories in post-stroke aphasia recovery
ideas.description: This study conducts intensive, multi-timescale longitudinal assessment of individuals with post-stroke aphasia, using a broad cognitive-linguistic battery and frequent multimodal neuroimaging. Instead of testing a specific dissociation model, it applies unsupervised learning and computational modeling to this high-dimensional dataset to identify naturally occurring clusters of recovery trajectories. The core aim is to discover how these empirically derived behavioral profiles map onto dynamic changes in neural network integrity, functional connectivity, and representational structure, thereby revealing the fundamental mechanisms of neuroplasticity that drive recovery.

ideas.title: Testing the Epiphenomenal Justification Hypothesis via Cognitive Neuroscience Methods
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that conscious reasoning is causally relevant to moral judgment, proposing it may be purely post-hoc. Drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience's debate on whether conscious experience is causal or epiphenomenal, we adapt its methodological solution. In that field, researchers used precise timing (e.g., Libet experiments) and neural correlates to test if brain activity precedes conscious awareness of intention. Adapted here, the research would use high-temporal-resolution tools (EEG/MEG) and behavioral response timing to measure the neural signatures of a moral decision (e.g., in emotion- or value-related brain regions) and compare their onset to the moment a subject can articulate a moral justification. If the neural decision consistently precedes the reportable justification, it would provide strong evidence that the conscious reasoning is a post-hoc rationalization with no causal role, directly testing the core hypothesis.

ideas.title: A Dual-Task ERP Framework to Behaviorally Anchor Real-Time Processing Signatures
ideas.description: This research integrates electrophysiology with a secondary reaction-time probe task to disambiguate the neural correlates of algorithmic and heuristic comprehension. By randomly interrupting sentence processing with simple auditory or visual probes at critical latencies, the system obtains a direct, time-resolved behavioral measure of cognitive load and resource allocation concurrent with ERP recording. The probe RT time-series serves as an independent validator to ground observed ERP components (like N400, P600) in a specific processing mode, moving beyond correlation to a more mechanistic account. This combined approach allows for the triangulation of temporal signatures that are robustly predictive of comprehension strategy shifts.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Computational Strategy Space: A Falsifiable Model of Processing Signatures
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond the qualitative continuum claim by developing a formal, quantifiable model of cognitive processing strategies. It defines a 'processing signature' as a point in a multi-dimensional space spanned by computationally grounded metrics, such as invariance learning rate and working memory recursion depth, which can be measured experimentally. Using a large-scale benchmark across diverse tasks and agent types (humans, AI models, neurological patients), it employs manifold learning techniques to empirically map the distribution of strategies. This allows for a direct test of the continuum hypothesis—searching for gaps or clusters—and identifies the fundamental trade-offs (e.g., speed vs. flexibility) that explain an agent's location in the strategy space.

ideas.title: Neural and Physiological Signatures of Heuristic Primacy and Algorithmic Switching
ideas.description: This research program uses high-precision psychophysiological and neural monitoring (e.g., EEG, pupillometry, cardiac measures) to test the hypothesis that heuristic processing is the brain's metabolically efficient default. It aims to identify a specific neural and physiological signature—characterized by lower cognitive load and automaticity—associated with initial heuristic application. The core innovation is to then pinpoint the exact moment and physiological triggers (e.g., conflict detection, surprise, increased arousal) that cause this default system to fail and recruit slower, algorithmic processing, thereby mapping the dynamic switch between cognitive modes onto a real-time biological substrate.

ideas.title: Adversarial Task Design to Decouple Task Demands from Cognitive Architecture
ideas.description: This research program uses formal modeling and adversarial design to empirically test whether behavioral dissociations are driven by task demands or underlying architecture. It constructs competing computational models representing the single-architecture and multiple-architecture hypotheses, then uses these models to generate predictions for novel, critical 'adversarial tasks.' By implementing these tasks and comparing model predictions to human behavioral and neural data, the approach creates a strong, falsifiable test. The outcome provides concrete evidence either for a flexible, demand-driven system or for the existence of distinct architectural components, moving beyond untestable theoretical speculation.

ideas.title: ERP Signatures of Cognitive Modes: A Signal Processing Approach from Seismology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from seismology, where different wave types with distinct temporal and spectral signatures are resolved from complex time-series data to understand geophysical events. The analogous problem of distinguishing algorithmic from heuristic comprehension in real time is addressed by applying similar signal processing techniques to ERP data. Instead of relying solely on ERP component latencies and amplitudes, the adapted method uses time-frequency analysis (e.g., wavelet transforms) to decompose the EEG signal. This allows for the characterization of distinct 'cognitive wave modes'—specifically, the oscillatory power and phase coherence within frequency bands like theta (linked to controlled, algorithmic processing) and alpha/beta (linked to heuristic, predictive processing). By identifying the temporal evolution of these spectral signatures during comprehension tasks, the method can time-resolve the dynamic interplay or switching between processing modes with greater precision than traditional ERP analysis alone.

ideas.title: Discourse-Level Dual-Route Parsing: A Computational Framework for Modeling Algorithmic vs. Heuristic Comprehension
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the discourse-level scope of Idea A with the formal modeling approach of Idea B to develop and test computational dual-route models of multi-sentence comprehension. The core synergy is using AI parsing architectures (e.g., transformer-based or symbolic-neural hybrids) to explicitly implement algorithmic (slow, structure-building) and heuristic (fast, pattern-matching) processing pathways for discourse phenomena like coherence resolution, inference generation, and narrative tracking. This allows for the generation of specific, quantitative predictions about behavioral and neural dissociations—such as reading times, error patterns, or fMRI activation—across different discourse structures and cognitive loads. The validated model would then serve as a mechanistic explanation for how and when each route dominates during naturalistic language understanding.

ideas.title: Decoding Dissociation via Multi-Modal Physiological Network Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-variable, network-based approach from systems ecology to study the dissociation of conscious experience. In ecology, system stability is understood by monitoring multiple interacting variables simultaneously to model complex dynamics. Adapted to the lab, this involves inducing a controlled dissociative state (e.g., via a sensory conflict paradigm) while conducting precise, concurrent monitoring of a network of neural (EEG, fMRI) and physiological (EDA, HRV, pupillometry, EMG) measures. The solution is to analyze these multi-modal data streams not in isolation, but as an interacting network, using techniques like dynamic causal modeling or network connectivity analysis to identify the specific cross-system dysregulation signature that constitutes the physiological 'architecture' of a dissociative episode.

ideas.title: Adapting Immunological Detection Paradigms to Map Unconscious Moral Appraisal Pathways
ideas.description: This research adapts solution strategies from immunology, where precise, unconscious detection pathways were mapped using reporter assays and knockout models, to the domain of moral cognition. The goal is to identify the specific cognitive processes of the unconscious system driving moral judgments. The adapted approach involves: 1) Designing 'reporter assays' using morally charged stimuli with carefully manipulated features (akin to antigens) and using high-temporal-resolution measures (e.g., very early event-related potentials like N1/P1, or startle reflex) as the output signal to trace the initial unconscious appraisal. 2) Implementing 'cognitive knockout' paradigms using neuromodulation (e.g., TMS) to temporarily inhibit brain regions hypothesized to be part of the initial appraisal network (e.g., amygdala, ventromedial prefrontal cortex, anterior insula), testing which disruptions halt the automatic moral evaluation. This combined method allows for a functional dissection of the rapid, unconscious appraisal system, moving beyond correlation to establish necessary components and their sequence in forming a moral intuition.

ideas.title: A Developmental Computational Model of Moral Dissociation
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'dual-route' model from reading acquisition to study the development of moral dissociation. In reading, separate neural pathways for phonological decoding and lexical recognition develop at different rates and can dissociate, causing specific deficits. Analogously, we propose a developmental computational model of moral cognition with an intuitive affective route (for quick moral judgments) and a deliberative justificatory route (for reasoned explanations). The study would examine how the maturation, integration, and relative strength of these routes change from childhood through adulthood, using behavioral tasks, neuroimaging, and computational modeling to predict when and why a person's moral judgment and their subsequent justification for it will diverge.

ideas.title: Triangulating moral conflict with validated neural process-tracing and computational modeling
ideas.description: This redesigned system first establishes a validated, neurally-grounded signature of decision conflict by using EEG/MEG to measure mid-frontal theta oscillations during incentivized choices with known, quantifiable value conflict (e.g., difficult perceptual or economic gambles). It then employs this neural signature as a ground-truth temporal marker of conflict during subsequent moral decision tasks, where it is recorded concurrently with mouse-tracking and explicit trial-by-trial reports of subjective conflict. Finally, a computational model integrates the neural, behavioral, and self-report data streams to disambiguate and temporally decompose the sources of conflict within moral judgments.

ideas.title: Longitudinal tracking of processing dissociations via ecological momentary assessment and computational phenotyping
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from psychiatry and affective science, where Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) and computational phenotyping are used to track the dynamic evolution of mood disorders. The analogous solution involves collecting frequent, real-time symptom data in natural environments to model state transitions and recovery mechanisms. Adapted to aphasia research, the idea proposes using high-frequency, ecologically valid language sampling (e.g., via smartphone app prompts for picture naming or sentence completion) to collect dense longitudinal data on algorithmic and heuristic processing dissociations during recovery. Computational phenotyping methods, such as time-series analysis and state-space modeling, would then be applied to this data to map the dynamic trajectories of these dissociations. The goal is to reveal the precise timing, contextual triggers, and underlying neuroplastic mechanisms (e.g., network reorganization, compensation) that govern shifts between processing states, offering a fine-grained, mechanistic view of language recovery plasticity.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Process Model to Decompose Strategy-Dependent Sensitivity and Bias
ideas.description: This research integrates Signal Detection Theory within a hierarchical Bayesian process model to formally quantify how task demands and individual differences shift the weighting between cognitive processing modes. The model jointly infers the sensitivity (d') and criterion (c) associated with putative algorithmic and heuristic strategies, as well as the probability of deploying each strategy on a trial-by-trial basis, from behavioral data. This approach overcomes the fatal flaw by not requiring pure, separable internal signals, instead modeling the observed data as a probabilistic mixture of processes with different SDT properties. The result is a robust, computationally explicit framework for dissociating processing pathways and their qualitative (bias) and quantitative (sensitivity) impacts on decision-making.

ideas.title: Architecture-Agnostic Behavioral Alignment for Testing Parsing Dissociations
ideas.description: This research develops a formal framework to test the dual-route parsing hypothesis without presupposing a specific computational architecture. It begins by defining a set of quantitative, model-independent behavioral benchmarks derived from the predicted dissociation, such as specific error trade-offs and robustness profiles. A diverse suite of computational parsers, from purely symbolic to end-to-end neural models, are then evaluated against these benchmarks on identical psycholinguistic tasks. The outcome is a rigorous, falsifiable comparison that identifies which class of computational principles—not just which pre-existing model—best accounts for human parsing behavior, providing stronger evidence for or against the proposed dissociation.

ideas.title: Inverse problem: Predict emotion from capture
ideas.description: Use the strength and pattern of attentional capture to infer the emotional state of the observer.
ideas.sourceTitle: Lexical selection is not by competition: a reinterpretation of semantic interference and facilitation effects in the picture-word interference paradigm.

ideas.title: Aphasia Subtype Dissociations: A Fault Diagnosis Framework
ideas.description: This research adapts the fault diagnosis methodology from engineering systems to map dissociations in aphasia. The analogous field is Engineering Systems & Fault Diagnosis, where complex systems are decomposed into functional modules, and specific input-output probes are used to isolate faults as either algorithmic (rule-based processing failure) or heuristic (contextual/associative processing failure). The adapted idea treats the language network as a system of interconnected functional modules (e.g., for syntax, lexicon, phonology). By designing and administering a targeted battery of linguistic 'probe' tasks that stress algorithmic versus heuristic processes within each module to individuals with Broca's, Wernicke's, and conduction aphasia, the research aims to generate a precise 'fault map' for each subtype. This map would dissect each syndrome's core deficit, identifying the specific impaired module(s) and characterizing the nature of the impairment as algorithmic, heuristic, or a connectivity issue, thereby providing a more mechanistic and computationally grounded taxonomy of aphasia.

ideas.title: Decoupling Verbal Report Fidelity from Process Awareness in Decision-Making
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that an inability to justify a decision or intuition implies the underlying cognitive process was unconscious. Drawing an analogy from Developmental Psychology's Theory of Mind research, which successfully decouples behavioral understanding from verbal explanation ability, we propose a similar methodological adaptation. The adapted idea involves designing decision-making tasks where the criterion for 'access' is not a verbal report but an implicit behavioral measure (e.g., the fidelity of a motor movement toward a choice, physiological arousal, or priming effects). By comparing the quality of implicit choice behavior with the quality of subsequent verbal justifications, we can test whether justification failure stems from a genuine lack of conscious access to the decision process or merely from a lack of verbal skill or meta-cognitive vocabulary to describe a consciously accessible process.

ideas.title: Single-Word Lexical Processing as a Minimal Unit for Algorithmic-Heuristic Dissociation
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from molecular biology, specifically protein folding research. In that field, to understand the complex rules governing how a linear chain of amino acids folds into a functional 3D structure, scientists reduced the problem to studying minimal systems—single-domain proteins or short peptides. This allowed them to isolate and dissect fundamental forces (like algorithmic rules of bond formation versus heuristic hydrophobic packing) without larger-scale interference. Adapting this solution, the research will scale down the study of algorithmic-heuristic dissociations in language to the minimal unit: the single word. The experimental paradigm will involve lexical processing tasks (e.g., primed lexical decision, masked morphological priming) designed to create conditions that favor either a rapid, holistic heuristic route or a slower, compositional algorithmic route. By measuring reaction times, accuracy, and neural correlates (e.g., EEG components like N400/P600 dissociations) in response to isolated word stimuli, the research aims to establish whether the core dual-process dichotomy is operative at the very foundation of lexical access, prior to the integration of syntactic or semantic context.

ideas.title: Apply a Cognitive Neuroscience Conflict-Adaptation Paradigm to the Moral Action-Inaction IAT
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience's study of implicit cognitive control. The analogous problem is measuring implicit conflict monitoring, solved by analyzing trial-sequence effects (conflict adaptation) in tasks like the Stroop. Adapting this solution, the research would apply a moral action-inaction Implicit Association Test (IAT) but employ a conflict-adaptation analytical framework. By examining how reaction times and accuracy on a given trial are influenced by the conflict level of the preceding trial, the study can measure dynamic, implicit adjustments in moral cognition, providing a richer metric of the cognitive system's sensitivity to the action-inaction distinction in harm contexts beyond static association strengths.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-level neural circuits

ideas.sourceTitle: Lexical selection is not by competition: a reinterpretation of semantic interference and facilitation effects in the picture-word interference paradigm.

ideas.title: Dissociating Algorithmic and Heuristic Processes in Real-Time Language Production Using Interference and Implicit Measures
ideas.description: This study investigates the algorithmic-heuristic dissociation in language production by using concurrent dual-task interference to selectively burden cognitive subsystems during speech planning and initiation. Implicit, real-time production metrics (e.g., vocal response latency, speech rate, and incremental syntactic choices) are collected to capture processes prior to conscious self-monitoring. These are directly compared to the same participants' performance on standardized comprehension dissociation tasks, and to their explicit judgments of their own produced utterances, thereby isolating production-specific effects from comprehension-based monitoring artifacts.

ideas.title: Linguistic-Neural Coupling Analysis of Justification Failure
ideas.description: This research program integrates computational linguistic analysis of written or spoken justifications with high-precision, lab-based psychophysiological monitoring (e.g., EEG, fMRI, pupillometry, skin conductance). Participants generate justifications for decisions or beliefs under controlled conditions where failure can be induced or observed. The key innovation is using real-time text analysis to identify emerging linguistic markers of failed reasoning (e.g., incoherence, repetitive phrasing, low semantic density) and using these markers as triggers to capture concurrent neural and physiological states. This allows researchers to move beyond correlation to model the dynamic interplay between language production patterns and the underlying neurocognitive processes during justification failure, testing whether specific linguistic features are direct outputs of identifiable cognitive or affective neural states.

ideas.title: Scale down to micro-feature semantics

ideas.title: Heuristics as the Cognitive Immune System: Algorithmic Recruitment as a Secondary Defense
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that algorithmic reasoning is the primary or normative mode of thought. By drawing an analogy to immunology, it posits that heuristic processing functions like the innate immune system: it is the default, fast, and efficient first line of cognitive defense for most judgments and decisions. Analogous to how the adaptive immune response is specifically mobilized only upon innate system failure, deliberate algorithmic reasoning is a secondary, resource-intensive process recruited only when heuristics generate signals of insufficiency, such as internal conflict, unexpected outcomes, or high stakes. The research program would involve identifying the cognitive and neural 'signals' that trigger this shift from heuristic-default to algorithmic-recruitment, fundamentally reframing the architecture of human reasoning.

ideas.title: Apply Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis from medical diagnostics to dissociate cognitive processing modes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) analysis framework, a cornerstone of signal detection theory in medical diagnostics and radiology, to the problem of dissociating cognitive processing modes. In the analogous medical field, ROC analysis separates a diagnostic test's inherent sensitivity (ability to detect disease) from the radiologist's decision bias (willingness to call a scan positive). The adapted research idea applies this to cognitive science by designing tasks where correct responses depend on deliberate algorithmic processing, while tempting errors arise from fast heuristic processing. By systematically varying conditions that influence the decision criterion (e.g., payoff matrices, instructions), researchers can plot an ROC curve for each participant. The area under this curve provides a bias-free measure of the sensitivity or strength of the algorithmic processing system, offering a rigorous quantitative tool to compare individual differences, clinical populations, or experimental manipulations in their reliance on two distinct cognitive processes.

ideas.title: Aphasic Dissociations as Ecological Phase Transitions
ideas.description: This idea challenges the computational metaphor by drawing an analogy from ecology and systems biology. In that field, systemic collapse (like a coral reef shifting to an algal-dominated state) is understood as an ecological phase transition, where the system's dynamics fundamentally reconfigure due to altered interactions and feedback loops, not a simple breakdown of a component. The analogous solution involves using network theory and non-linear dynamics to model resilience and tipping points. Adapting this, the research would reframe aphasic dissociations not as broken linguistic processors but as phase transitions within an embodied, ecological language system. Aphasia would be modeled as a shift in the stable state of a dynamic system tightly coupled with its environment (social, physical, neural), where neurological change alters critical interaction parameters, reconfiguring the entire functional landscape of communication—explaining dissociations through the new topology of accessible states rather than deleted computations.

ideas.title: Dual-route parsing modeled via ecological decision-making frameworks
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from behavioral ecology—the modeling of exploit-explore decision trade-offs in Optimal Foraging Theory—to the context of dual-route parsing in psycholinguistics. The analogous solution uses formal computational models (e.g., multi-armed bandits) to simulate how animals weigh a reliable but costly strategy against a cheap but uncertain one. The adapted idea formalizes the algorithmic parsing route as a high-precision, high-effort 'exploit' of grammatical rules and the heuristic route as a low-effort, probabilistic 'explore' of surface cues. By defining cognitive costs and uncertainty metrics within this framework, the model can simulate and generate testable predictions for when and why the human parser switches between these two processing routes, offering a rigorous computational test of the proposed dissociation.

ideas.title: Study the inverse problem: comprehension from production

ideas.title: Dynamic Parallel Processing: A Resource-Rational Integration of Heuristics and Algorithms
ideas.description: This framework proposes that heuristic and algorithmic processing are not primary and backup systems but are continuously active, parallel computational strategies. The cognitive system dynamically integrates their outputs based on a resource-rational calculus, weighing the expected cost of deeper computation against the adaptive sufficiency of a heuristic given contextual constraints like time, accuracy needs, and available cognitive resources. Research would employ computational modeling to formalize this integration and neurobehavioral experiments to identify the control signals that modulate the weighting of each process, moving beyond a simplistic failure-triggered switch.

ideas.title: Dissociating Verbal Articulation from Conscious Access: A Convergent Validity Approach
ideas.description: This research program systematically tests whether failed justifications stem from a lack of conscious access or merely a failure of verbal report. It uses a within-subjects design where participants perform a perceptual or decision-making task under conditions known to produce unconscious influence. Crucially, justifications are measured not only verbally but also through non-verbal, information-rich methods like cue-selection arrays or pattern arrangement. These measures are triangulated with independent behavioral and physiological indices of conscious awareness. By establishing whether poor justifications align with unconscious markers even when verbal demands are minimized, the design robustly tests the core claim that justification inability implies unconscious processing.

ideas.title: Discourse-Level Moral Justification Training: Testing for Processing Dissociations in Narrative Comprehension
ideas.description: This research investigates whether teaching a structured moral justification principle (e.g., the doctrine of double effect) induces a measurable shift from heuristic to more algorithmic processing during the comprehension of extended, morally complex narratives. Participants would be trained on the principle and then read multi-sentence discourses where the moral outcome is consistent or inconsistent with the trained rule, while their reading times, eye movements, or neural activity are tracked. The core test is whether post-training comprehension shows dissociations characteristic of algorithmic processing (e.g., rule-based integration, sensitivity to logical contradictions within the narrative) for scenarios where the rule applies, while heuristic processing (e.g., reliance on emotional valence, outcome bias) persists for untrained or rule-inapplicable scenarios. This directly tests if moral reasoning training rewires real-time discourse comprehension mechanisms, not just final judgments.
