ideas.title: Ecologically-Valid Capture of Dissociation: Bridging Lab-Induced States to Real-World Dynamics
ideas.description: This research integrates controlled laboratory assessment with intensive longitudinal field monitoring to overcome the ecological validity gap. In the lab, dissociation is induced using personalized, clinically-relevant stimuli (e.g., script-driven imagery) while collecting high-density EEG and precise psychophysiology. Participants then complete a 14-day ecological momentary assessment (EMA) protocol, wearing a wearable device that continuously measures heart rate variability and electrodermal activity, and responding to smartphone prompts about dissociative state and context. The design explicitly tests whether the neural and physiological signatures identified in the lab predict and map onto spontaneously occurring dissociative episodes in daily life, creating a robust, generalizable biomarker model.

ideas.title: A Continuum Model of Cognitive Processing: Adapting the Quantum-Classical Transition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the strict algorithmic-heuristic dichotomy by drawing an analogy from quantum physics, specifically the resolution of the quantum-classical boundary problem. The analogous solution from decoherence theory replaced a sharp, fundamental divide with a model of continuous transition based on environmental interaction. Adapted to cognitive science, this suggests modeling cognitive processing strategies on a continuum governed by a 'cognitive coherence' parameter. This continuous parameter would be influenced by contextual factors such as time constraints, available cognitive resources, and task familiarity, determining the degree of algorithmic control versus heuristic autonomy in any given cognitive operation, thereby dissolving the discrete systems dichotomy.

ideas.title: Computational Modeling of Heuristic-First Parsing with Algorithmic Fallback
ideas.description: This research direction develops formal computational models of language parsing where a fast, heuristic-based route serves as the primary processor, generating initial interpretations based on shallow cues and statistical regularities. The slower, algorithmic route is modeled as a resource-intensive fallback system, activated only when the heuristic output is flagged as unreliable or conflicts with contextual goals. By simulating this architecture, the models can generate testable predictions about when and why parsing failures or 'illusion' effects occur, and quantify the cognitive cost savings of the heuristic-default system. This provides a rigorous computational foundation for the theoretical reversal of the traditional algorithmic-primary view.

ideas.title: Massively Parallel, Culturally-Calibrated Model Fitting of Moral Parsing Algorithms
ideas.description: This research program deploys a massive, cross-cultural online dilemma battery to collect millions of moral judgments. These rich behavioral datasets are used not for simple hypothesis testing, but as high-resolution training and validation grounds for competing computational models of dual-route moral parsing (e.g., transformer-based vs. heuristic architectures). By fitting and comparing these models across diverse cultural and linguistic subgroups, the approach can computationally isolate and quantify how cultural priors, linguistic structures, and situational features modulate the engagement of algorithmic versus heuristic processing pathways in real-time moral decision-making.

ideas.title: Use Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) for Justification Texts
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) method from Systems and Reliability Engineering to the computational linguistic analysis of failed justifications. In the analogous field, FMEA is used to systematically catalog potential failures in a system, scoring them by severity, likelihood of occurrence, and ease of detection to prioritize remediation. Adapted to this research context, the method involves treating specific, computationally detectable linguistic patterns within justification texts (such as identifiable logical fallacies, hedges, or evidence gaps) as 'failure modes.' These patterns are then scored and ranked based on their calculated frequency (occurrence), their predicted impact on the argument's persuasiveness or logical soundness (severity), and how latent or overt they are to standard analysis (detection). This creates a structured, risk-prioritized taxonomy of justification failures, moving beyond simple pattern identification to a diagnostic framework that highlights the most critical and common flaws in reasoning.

ideas.title: The Operative Principles of Real-World Moral Justification
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond hypothetical trolley problems to investigate the role of the Doctrine of Double Effect (DDE) in real-world, high-stakes domains like end-of-life medical care, military targeting decisions, or public policy trade-offs. It employs a multi-method approach, combining behavioral observation, decision-tracking, and in-depth justification interviews with actors (e.g., clinicians, commanders) to identify the actual operative principles—such as outcome aversion, emotional salience, or rule compliance—that guide their choices. The core innovation is systematically analyzing the gap between these discovered drivers and the subsequent use of DDE-structured reasoning, testing if appeals to double effect causally influence action or primarily serve to legitimize decisions made on other grounds.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Moral Justification Gaps
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) solution from Health Psychology to scale moral psychology research from hypothetical dilemmas to actual behavior. In the analogous field, EMA solves the problem of studying real-world health behaviors by collecting in-the-moment, context-rich data via personal devices, minimizing retrospective distortion. The adapted research uses a similar smartphone-based protocol, triggering brief surveys after geolocation, calendar entries, or self-reports indicate a potential moral event, to capture the immediate action, moral judgment, and justification. This allows for the direct, longitudinal study of the judgment-justification gap as it occurs in daily life, linking it to real contexts, stressors, and social dynamics.

ideas.title: Culturally-stratified global sampling via multi-platform recruitment
ideas.description: This redesigned system moves beyond simple massive scaling on a few platforms. It begins by defining a target global sample structure based on cultural dimensions (e.g., individualism-collectivism, tightness-looseness), language families, and key demographics. Researchers then actively recruit participants through a coordinated mix of online panels, local academic partnerships, and targeted digital advertisements across diverse platforms and regions to meet precise strata quotas. This ensures the massive sample is demographically and culturally representative, not merely large, allowing for valid cross-cultural comparisons and generalizable findings about human moral psychology.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Distributed Sampling via a Federated Platform Protocol
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from astrophysics' use of distributed computing networks (e.g., Einstein@Home) to process massive datasets, this idea adapts the solution of a standardized, lightweight client protocol. The analogous problem involved harnessing globally distributed, heterogeneous computing power to analyze data from large-scale sensors (telescopes). The adapted research idea creates a 'Dilemma Protocol'—a simple, open API and embedding standard. This allows any online platform worldwide to voluntarily deploy individual dilemma items as micro-tasks to their user base. Each interaction submits only the response and essential, anonymized contextual metadata (e.g., inferred country/language of the host platform) to a central academic repository. This federated approach scales data collection to millions by distributing the sampling load across the entire web ecosystem, capturing more organic and culturally varied data than a single-platform deployment, while mitigating reliance on any one corporate gatekeeper.
